explanation: 'Skill' is usually countable when you mean specific abilities, so use the plural 'skills' in this general sense: 'learning skills', 'communication skills'. (assistant note: from the session summary, T2.)
correct_examples: He has strong learning skills. | Good communication skills are essential.
context: Describing the upside of training in new areas

my_sentence: I read some news about AI company.
correction: I read some news about an AI company.
explanation: When you talk about one, countable, singular thing, you must use an article. 'AI company' is a singular countable noun, so it needs 'an' (or 'the' if specific); leaving it bare is wrong. If you mean more than one, use the plural ('AI companies').
correct_examples: I read an article about an AI company. | There are many AI companies in the world.
context: Talking about what Jack did during the day (podcasts, AI companies)

my_sentence: I have some free time during my work, so I'm not busy.
correction: I have some free time at work, so I'm not busy.
explanation: Use 'at work' to mean 'while at one's job'. 'during my work' is an L1-style literal translation and sounds unnatural in English.
correct_examples: I have some free time at work. | She reads emails at work in the morning.
context: Saying he was not very busy that day

my_sentence: You can use them to your work and improve your efficiency.
correction: You can use them at work to improve your efficiency.
explanation: 'use something at work' is the correct collocation for applying a skill in your job. 'to your work' is the wrong preposition here.
correct_examples: You can use these skills at work. | I apply what I learn at work every day.
context: Explaining how training helps you keep a job

my_sentence: Many years later you can become an expert in your industry.
correction: Many years later you can become an expert in your career.
explanation: Here you mean the field of work you personally build over time, so 'career' fits better than 'industry'. 'industry' refers to a whole economic sector, not your individual professional path.
correct_examples: After ten years she became an expert in her career. | He built a successful career in software.
context: Explaining how learning skills helps you keep a job

my_sentence: If someone can arrive on time, he did all her tasks on time, so we can arrange tasks to him.
correction: If someone can finish his or her tasks on time, he or she can be responsible for his or her tasks.
explanation: When the subject is 'someone', keep the pronouns consistent: use 'he or she' / 'his or her' throughout, not a mix of 'he', 'her', 'him'. Don't switch gender or person within the same sentence.
correct_examples: If someone finishes his or her work on time, he or she is reliable. | Everyone should bring his or her own laptop.
context: Listing strategies for keeping a job (arriving on time)

my_sentence: Keeping a good attitude is useful for keeping a job.
correction: Keeping a good attitude is useful for having a job.
explanation: Avoid repeating the same word ('keeping ... keeping') in one sentence. A gerund like 'keeping' usually has no exact synonym, so the repetition is awkward; replace the second one with 'having a job'.
correct_examples: Keeping a good attitude is useful for having a job. | Staying calm is important for doing good work.
context: Writing strategies for keeping a job

my_sentence: It can help you keep professional in front of your customers.
correction: It can help you be professional in front of your customers.
explanation: The natural collocation is 'be professional', not 'keep professional'. ('keep' also repeats the earlier 'keeping', which the teacher was avoiding.)
correct_examples: A good attitude helps you be professional with clients. | Try to be professional in meetings.
context: Explaining why a good attitude helps at work

my_sentence: Aside from it, asking questions is useful... When you join a new group you will meet a lot of problems and it can help you a lot.
correction: Aside from that, asking questions is useful... When you join a new group you will meet a lot of problems and that can help you a lot.
explanation: Use the demonstrative pronoun 'that' (not 'it') when you refer back to a whole idea or the entire situation you just described. 'it' points to one specific noun; 'that' wraps up everything previously said.
correct_examples: I lost my keys, and that ruined my morning. | Aside from that, we should also test the code.
context: Listing strategies for keeping a job (asking questions)

my_sentence: For some functions that AI can replace, and for other...
correction: For some functions that AI can replace, it can help you navigate and find your items.
explanation: 'For some functions that AI can replace' is only a fragment (a prepositional phrase + relative clause), not a complete sentence. After the comma you must add a complete independent clause with its own subject and verb to finish the idea.
correct_examples: For the tasks that AI can handle, it works faster than people. | In the cases that AI can solve, it saves a lot of time.
context: Discussing which functions AI can replace in a store

my_sentence: AI can use their camera to take some photos.
correction: AI can use its camera to take some photos.
explanation: 'AI' is singular and not a person, so the possessive is 'its', not 'their'. Match the pronoun to the singular, non-human subject.
correct_examples: The robot uses its camera to scan items. | The system saves its data automatically.
context: Explaining how AI checks item quality with photos

my_sentence: We need to through the bad meat into the garbage.
correction: We need to throw the bad meat into the garbage.
explanation: 'throw' (toss away, /θroʊ/) and 'through' (passing across, /θruː/) sound and spell differently and have different meanings. You wanted 'throw away', so use 'throw'.
correct_examples: Please throw the trash in the bin. | We walked through the park.
context: Talking about AI checking food/item quality

my_sentence: We have our own model to help us finish a specific scenario.
correction: We have our own model to help us finish a specific task.
explanation: What the model completes is a 'task' (a specific job/operation), not a 'scenario' (a situation or scene). Use 'task' for something to be done.
correct_examples: We trained a model for a specific task. | The tool automates repetitive tasks.
context: Explaining the company's own AI model

my_sentence: Nowadays some country try to use AI technology to solve cases.
correction: Nowadays some countries try to use AI technology to solve cases.
explanation: 'some' with a countable noun takes the plural form: 'some countries'. The verb 'try' is then correct for the plural subject.
correct_examples: Some countries have banned the app. | Some companies use AI to screen resumes.
context: Talking about countries using AI to solve criminal cases

my_sentence: He or she just needs to take a breath and think a little.
correction: He or she just needs to calm down and think about their behavior a little.
explanation: To describe controlling emotions, 'calm down' (or 'take a breather') is the natural phrase. 'take a breath' is literal (one inhale) and doesn't carry the meaning of settling your mood.
correct_examples: When you get angry, calm down before you reply. | Take a breather and think it over.
context: How someone keeps a good attitude toward others

my_sentence: Showing a good manner is a good attitude.
correction: Showing good manners is a good attitude.
explanation: In the sense of polite social behavior, 'manners' is always plural and takes no article: 'good manners'. The singular 'a manner' means 'a way of doing something', which is not what you mean here.
correct_examples: Children should learn good manners. | He always shows good manners at the table.
context: Discussing manners as part of a good attitude

my_sentence: If you speak, you will choke.
correction: If you speak, you can choke.
explanation: Use 'can' for possibility, not 'will'. Speaking with a full mouth doesn't guarantee choking; it only makes it possible, so 'can choke' is correct.
correct_examples: If you eat too fast, you can choke. | If you touch it, you can get burned.
context: Explaining table manners (not talking with a full mouth)

my_sentence: If you point out it in public, he or she will hate you.
correction: If you point it out in public, he or she will hate you.
explanation: With a separable phrasal verb like 'point out', a pronoun object must go between the verb and the particle: 'point it out', never 'point out it'. (A full noun can go either side: 'point out the mistake' / 'point the mistake out'.)
correct_examples: Don't point it out in front of everyone. | She turned it off before leaving.
context: Talking about correcting someone's behavior privately vs. publicly

my_sentence: Even though you respect for them and you call them older, they will feel angry.
correction: Even though you respect them and you call them older, they will feel angry.
explanation: The verb 'respect' takes a direct object with no preposition: 'respect them', not 'respect for them'. (The noun 'respect for someone' does take 'for', but the verb does not.)
correct_examples: I respect my teachers. | They respect each other's opinions.
context: Discussing how addressing women as 'older' can offend

my_sentence: We can ask many questions for free from them.
correction: We can ask many questions freely from them.
explanation: To mean 'without hesitation / openly', use the adverb 'freely' to modify the verb 'ask'. 'for free' means 'without paying', which is not what you mean here.
correct_examples: You can ask me anything freely. | Speak freely; no one will judge you.
context: Explaining how building relationships lets you ask questions

my_sentence: We need to find the core members for a project, because there are too many people, we cannot build a good connection with them.
correction: Since there are too many people, we cannot build a good connection with them.
explanation: When the reason clause comes first, 'Since ...' is the natural linker, followed by a comma and the main clause. Putting 'because' mid-sentence here created a run-on; lead with 'Since' to give the cause cleanly.
correct_examples: Since there are too many people, we can't talk to everyone. | Since it was raining, we stayed inside.
context: Explaining why you must find the core members of a project

my_sentence: Different projects will have opposing groups.
correction: Different projects will not have similar groups.
explanation: 'opposing' means 'against / in conflict', which reverses your meaning. You meant that each project has its own different team, so say the projects 'will not have similar groups'.
correct_examples: Different projects will not have the same team. | Each project has its own group of people.
context: Explaining that each project has its own separate team

my_sentence: I can write a little but can't write a lot.
correction: I can write a little, but not a lot.
explanation: Don't repeat 'write'. Once the verb is established, drop the second 'can't write' and use the concise contrast 'but not a lot'.
correct_examples: I can walk a little, but not a lot. | She speaks some French, but not fluently.
context: Telling the teacher about his recovering hand

my_sentence: Today was a holiday, so I can with my family go some places to play.
correction: Today was a holiday, so I was able to go to some places with my family.
explanation: Don't split the verb phrase with 'with my family'. Keep 'go to some places' together and put 'with my family' after it. Use 'was able to' for a past ability/opportunity.
correct_examples: I was able to visit several places with my family. | We went to the park with our kids.
context: Talking about a holiday outing

my_sentence: The background looks some different.
correction: The background looks different.
explanation: Don't use 'some' to soften an adjective like 'different'. Either say 'looks different' or 'looks a bit/a little different'.
correct_examples: Your room looks different today. | It looks a little different from before.
context: The teacher noticing Jack's hotel background

my_sentence: One of my coworkers who always later to the company.
correction: One of my coworkers who was always late.
explanation: 'Later' is the comparative form; for the simple quality use the base adjective 'late'. The relative clause also needs a verb: 'who was always late'.
correct_examples: He was always late for work. | The colleague who was often late got a warning.
context: Practising relative clauses with a business example

my_sentence: One of my coworkers is going to fly to the Tokyo next month.
correction: One of my coworkers is going to fly to Tokyo next month.
explanation: Don't use 'the' before most city names. There is only one Tokyo, so it takes no article.
correct_examples: She flew to Tokyo last week. | We're traveling to Paris in June.
context: Practising relative clauses

my_sentence: My manager and I are going to be visit our customer.
correction: My manager and I are going to visit our customer.
explanation: After 'be going to' use the base verb directly: 'are going to visit'. Don't insert 'be' before the main verb. (Adding 'be' would make it passive, where the subject receives the action.)
correct_examples: We are going to visit the client tomorrow. | They are going to launch the product soon.
context: Practising relative clauses with company roles

my_sentence: If someone is ambitious, he or she will have a big dream that need to be achieved.
correction: If someone is ambitious, he or she will have a big dream that he or she needs to achieve.
explanation: Inside the relative clause give it a subject and an agreeing verb: 'that he or she needs to achieve'. 'That need' is missing the subject and the third-person -s.
correct_examples: She has a goal that she wants to achieve. | It's a target that the team needs to reach.
context: Building sentences about an ambitious person

my_sentence: I want to say something there.
correction: I want to see something there.
explanation: You meant 'see' (to observe), but it came out as 'say' (to speak). Keep the vowels distinct: 'see' /iː/ vs 'say' /eɪ/.
correct_examples: I want to see how people live there. | I'd like to see it for myself.
context: Explaining curiosity about North Korea

my_sentence: I want to go to a develop country, not a developing country.
correction: I want to go to a developed country.
explanation: Use the adjective 'developed' (past participle) for a rich, advanced country. 'Develop' is the base verb; 'developing' describes a country still growing.
correct_examples: Japan is a developed country. | They moved to a developed nation for better pay.
context: Why Jack wants to travel abroad

my_sentence: How about their life, their situations?
correction: How about their lives and their situations?
explanation: For several people use the plural 'lives' (life → lives). Match the plural already used in 'situations'.
correct_examples: Their lives changed completely. | We discussed their lives and challenges.
context: Curiosity about people's lives in North Korea

my_sentence: It will not danger.
correction: It will not be dangerous.
explanation: 'Danger' is a noun; the adjective is 'dangerous'. After 'will not' you need 'be' plus the adjective: 'will not be dangerous'.
correct_examples: It won't be dangerous. | The trip will be safe.
context: Reassuring the teacher the trip is safe

my_sentence: Someone who is proactive can succeed easily than others.
correction: Someone who is proactive can succeed more easily than others.
explanation: When comparing with 'than', the adverb 'easily' needs the comparative form 'more easily'. Most two-syllable+ adverbs form the comparative with 'more'.
correct_examples: He learns more easily than his peers. | They finished more quickly than expected.
context: Describing a proactive person

my_sentence: If someone is punctual, he or she does not want to late.
correction: If someone is punctual, he or she does not want to be late.
explanation: 'Late' is an adjective, so it needs 'be': 'want to be late'. You can't follow 'to' directly with an adjective.
correct_examples: She doesn't want to be late. | I try not to be late for meetings.
context: Describing a punctual person

my_sentence: If someone is diligent, he or she tried to finish his or her work well.
correction: If someone is diligent, he or she finishes his or her work well.
explanation: For a general, always-true description use the simple present ('finishes'), not the past 'tried'. Match the present tense of 'is' in the first clause.
correct_examples: A diligent person finishes tasks carefully. | She always completes her work on time.
context: Describing a diligent person

my_sentence: If someone is gentle, he or she can treat others well.
correction: If someone is gentle, he or she treats others well.
explanation: For a definite, characteristic fact, use the simple present without a modal. Only use 'can/will/may' when there's possibility or future meaning, not for a sure statement.
correct_examples: A gentle person treats others kindly. | He speaks softly and respects everyone.
context: The teacher's rule about not overusing modal verbs for sure statements

my_sentence: One of my coworkers who is optimistic can face many challenges at work, and he will not give up.
correction: One of my coworkers who is optimistic can face many challenges at work, and he does not give up easily.
explanation: Use 'will' only for future events. For a general habit or trait, use the simple present 'does not give up'.
correct_examples: He never gives up easily. | She doesn't quit when things get hard.
context: Describing an optimistic coworker

my_sentence: One of my friends who is a pessimistic likes to stay at home all day.
correction: One of my friends who is pessimistic likes to stay at home all day.
explanation: Don't put 'a' before an adjective. Either use the adjective alone ('is pessimistic') or 'a' + noun ('is a pessimist').
correct_examples: He is pessimistic about the future. | She is a pessimist by nature.
context: Describing a pessimistic friend

my_sentence: The boy who lived in the same neighbourhood with me is ambitious.
correction: The boy who lived in the same neighbourhood with me was ambitious.
explanation: Keep the tense consistent. Since 'lived' is past, use 'was', not the present 'is'.
correct_examples: The boy who lived next door was very kind. | My old classmate who sat beside me was funny.
context: Describing an ambitious boy from the past

my_sentence: Even though the boy is diligent, couldn't get a high score at school.
correction: Even though the boy is diligent, he couldn't get a high score at school.
explanation: The main clause needs a subject. Add the pronoun 'he' before 'couldn't'.
correct_examples: Although she studied hard, she failed the test. | Even though he tried, he didn't win.
context: Describing a diligent boy

my_sentence: My volume is too slow, I need to make it louder.
correction: My volume is too soft (too low), I need to make it louder.
explanation: 'Slow' is the opposite of 'fast' (speed). For a quiet sound use 'soft' or 'low'; its opposite is 'loud'.
correct_examples: Your voice is too soft; please speak up. | Turn it up — the volume is too low.
context: The teacher asking Jack to speak louder

my_sentence: Mrs Mary is very honest and she was not greedy anymore.
correction: Mrs Mary is not greedy anymore; she is now an honest person.
explanation: Keep the tense consistent — don't mix present 'is' with past 'was' in the same description. 'Anymore' means 'no longer / not at present', so it goes with the present tense.
correct_examples: He doesn't smoke anymore; he is much healthier now. | She is not shy anymore.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'greedy'

my_sentence: Mr White is a snob person.
correction: Mr White is a snob. / Mr White is a snobbish person.
explanation: 'Snob' is a noun, so it can't directly modify 'person'. Either use the noun alone ('is a snob') or use the adjective form ('a snobbish person').
correct_examples: He is a snob. | She has a snobbish attitude.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'snob'

my_sentence: No one become his real friend.
correction: No one becomes his real friend.
explanation: 'No one' is singular (like 'he/she'), so the present-tense verb takes -s: 'becomes'.
correct_examples: No one knows the answer. | Nobody wants to help him.
context: Describing a snob nobody befriends

my_sentence: Last week I met a stranger who is a snob.
correction: Last week I met a stranger who was a snob, and I couldn't communicate with him, so I left him right away.
explanation: 'Last week' marks past time, so use the past tense 'was', not the present 'is', inside the relative clause.
correct_examples: Last week I met a man who was very rude. | Yesterday I saw a stranger who was lost.
context: Describing a rude stranger

my_sentence: He always looked down other people at work.
correction: He always looks down on other people at work.
explanation: The phrasal verb is 'look down on someone' (to think you are better than them); don't drop 'on'. For a general trait use the present 'looks'.
correct_examples: She looks down on people who are poor. | Don't look down on others.
context: Describing a frivolous, arrogant coworker

my_sentence: Many professors and students will come to here to join.
correction: Many professors and students will come here to join.
explanation: 'Here' and 'there' are adverbs of place and don't take 'to'. Say 'come here', not 'come to here'.
correct_examples: Come here, please. | Many people came here for the event.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'discourse'

my_sentence: Many people who lived in the same neighbourhood with Mr John think he is arrogant and conceited.
correction: Many people who lived in the same neighbourhood as Mr John thought he was arrogant and conceited.
explanation: Match the tenses: since 'lived' is past, use 'thought' and 'was', not the present 'think/is'. (Also 'the same ... as', not 'with'.)
correct_examples: The people who worked with him thought he was kind. | Those who lived there said it was quiet.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'conceited'

my_sentence: We need to keep a bottom line so I did not hurt others.
correction: We need to keep a bottom line so as not to hurt others.
explanation: To express purpose ('in order not to'), use 'so as not to + base verb', not 'so I did not'.
correct_examples: He spoke softly so as not to wake the baby. | We left early so as not to be late.
context: Making a sentence with 'bottom line'

my_sentence: I have to hard work to finish my dream.
correction: I have to work hard to finish my dream.
explanation: 'Work' is the verb and 'hard' is the adverb after it: 'work hard'. 'Hard work' is a noun phrase and can't follow 'have to'. (You made this same slip the next day — watch for it.)
correct_examples: I have to work hard to reach my goal. | They worked hard for years.
context: Talking about achieving his dreams with enough money

my_sentence: At least they need to graduate from famous college.
correction: At least they need to graduate from a famous college. / ...from famous colleges.
explanation: 'College' is countable. Use 'a famous college' for one, or the plural 'famous colleges' for several. Don't leave a singular countable noun bare.
correct_examples: She graduated from a famous college. | They attended famous colleges abroad.
context: Discussing high-paying jobs and education

my_sentence: When I came to Japan I can earn two salaries during that time.
correction: When I came to Japan I could earn two salaries during that time.
explanation: For a past ability use the past modal 'could', not the present 'can'. The time phrase 'during that time' is in the past.
correct_examples: Back then I could save a lot. | When I lived there, I could speak the language.
context: Earning two salaries while working in Japan

my_sentence: There are many people who like him come out nowadays.
correction: There are many people who are like him appearing nowadays.
explanation: To mean 'similar to him', use 'be like': 'who are like him' (don't drop 'are'). Also, for a person emerging/becoming known, use 'appear', not 'come out'.
correct_examples: There are many leaders who are like her today. | New talents appear every year.
context: Whether another businessman like Jack Ma could emerge

my_sentence: Taobao belong to Jack Ma.
correction: Taobao belongs to Jack Ma.
explanation: 'Taobao' is a singular subject, so the present-tense verb takes -s: 'belongs'.
correct_examples: This account belongs to me. | The brand belongs to a big company.
context: Talking about Jack Ma's companies

my_sentence: His net worth is about 28 billion dollar.
correction: His net worth is about 28 billion dollars.
explanation: With a plural amount, use 'dollars'. After a number greater than one the countable noun must be plural.
correct_examples: It costs 50 dollars. | He is worth billions of dollars.
context: Discussing a businessman's net worth

my_sentence: I didn't search the informations.
correction: I didn't search for this information.
explanation: 'Information' is uncountable — it has no plural 'informations' and takes no 'a'. Also use 'search for' when you mean to look something up.
correct_examples: I searched for information online. | There isn't much information about it.
context: Jack saying he hadn't looked something up

my_sentence: Many products from the US and UK made in China.
correction: Many products from the US and UK were made in China.
explanation: This is passive voice and needs 'be' + past participle: 'were made'. Don't drop the auxiliary 'were'.
correct_examples: These goods were made in China. | The phone was designed in the US but made in China.
context: Talking about where products are manufactured

my_sentence: Maybe we can talk it later.
correction: Maybe we can talk about it later.
explanation: Use 'talk about something'. The verb 'talk' needs the preposition 'about' before the topic.
correct_examples: Let's talk about it tomorrow. | We talked about the project.
context: Ending the class

my_sentence: They become a rich, not from themselves.
correction: They became rich, not by themselves.
explanation: Use the past tense 'became' for a completed state in the past, and don't put 'a' before the adjective 'rich' (it is not a singular countable noun here). Also say 'by themselves' (= through their own effort), not 'from themselves'.
correct_examples: He became rich through hard work, not by inheritance. | They got rich on their own.
context: Talking about rich people who inherited money

my_sentence: Their money wasn't earned by themselves. It was from their family members.
correction: Their money wasn't earned by themselves, but it was from their family members.
explanation: Instead of cutting one idea into two short clipped sentences, join the contrast with a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so). 'but' shows contrast between the two ideas.
correct_examples: He didn't earn it himself, but he inherited it from his parents. | She works hard, yet she still struggles to save.
context: The teacher pushing Jack to connect clauses with conjunctions instead of stopping mid-sentence

my_sentence: They expect them keep stability.
correction: They expect their children to keep the stability.
explanation: Use a clear noun/pronoun ('their children') so it's obvious who you mean, and the structure 'expect someone TO do something' needs the infinitive marker 'to'. The verb 'expect' cannot be followed directly by a bare verb.
correct_examples: Parents expect their children to behave well. | We expect the team to finish on time.
context: What rich parents want for their children

my_sentence: She went to my house for many weeks.
correction: She came to my house for many weeks.
explanation: Use 'come' for movement toward the speaker or the speaker's location, and 'go' for movement away. Since the house is where you (the speaker) are, your mother 'came' to it.
correct_examples: My mother came to visit me last week. | Please come to my place this weekend.
context: Jack's mother visiting his home

my_sentence: Two weeks later it's the summer vacation, so maybe my daughter will go to my hometown.
correction: Two weeks later it will be the summer vacation, so maybe she will go to my hometown.
explanation: Once 'my daughter' has been introduced, refer back to her with the pronoun 'she' instead of repeating the full noun. Repeating the noun every time sounds unnatural.
correct_examples: My daughter is on holiday, so she will visit her grandmother. | When my son finishes school, he will travel.
context: Plans for his daughter's summer holiday

my_sentence: I'm not sure if she likes or not.
correction: I'm not sure if she likes it or not.
explanation: The transitive verb 'like' needs an object. Add the object pronoun 'it' to refer to the idea (going to the hometown): 'whether she likes it or not'.
correct_examples: I don't know if she likes it or not. | Try the food and see whether you like it.
context: Whether his daughter will enjoy the trip

my_sentence: It was impossible for me to do these things when I was children.
correction: It was impossible for me to do these things when I was a child.
explanation: 'I' is one person, so use the singular 'a child', not the plural 'children'. Subject and complement must agree in number.
correct_examples: When I was a child, I loved drawing. | She was a quiet kid back then.
context: Jack's childhood dream of being rich

my_sentence: It's just a dream and I can't do anything to make money.
correction: It was just a dream, and I couldn't do anything to make money.
explanation: You are describing the past (your childhood), so use past tense throughout: 'was' and 'couldn't', not 'is' and 'can't'.
correct_examples: It was just a dream back then. | I couldn't afford it when I was young.
context: Reflecting on childhood ambitions

my_sentence: And you worked for your family, you couldn't get money.
correction: And I worked for my family, so I couldn't get my own money.
explanation: You are talking about your own experience, so keep the subject consistent as 'I/my', not 'you/your'. Switching to 'you' makes it sound general and confusing.
correct_examples: I worked for my family, so I earned no money of my own. | When I helped at home, I wasn't paid.
context: Working for the family as a child without pay

my_sentence: They want their children to stay stability.
correction: They want their children to be stable.
explanation: After 'to be/to stay' you need an adjective, not a noun. Use the adjective 'stable', not the noun 'stability'.
correct_examples: They want their children to be stable and safe. | He just wants to stay calm under pressure.
context: What rich parents want for their kids

my_sentence: Their life was very interesting.
correction: Their lives would be very interesting.
explanation: This is a hypothetical situation (if they could do whatever they like), so use 'would be', not the simple past 'was'. Also, for several people use the plural 'lives'.
correct_examples: If they had that freedom, their lives would be exciting. | Their lives would be easier with more money.
context: Imagining the life of rich children with no responsibilities

my_sentence: They need to hard work to finish their tasks.
correction: They need to work hard to finish their tasks.
explanation: 'Work' is the verb and 'hard' is an adverb that follows it: 'work hard'. 'Hard work' is a noun phrase and cannot follow 'need to'.
correct_examples: You have to work hard to succeed. | They worked hard all year.
context: Listing what rich people do

my_sentence: For a business, the most important thing is stabilizer.
correction: For a business, the most important thing is stability.
explanation: 'Stability' is the noun for the quality of being steady. A 'stabilizer' is a device or substance that keeps something steady. You wanted the abstract noun 'stability'.
correct_examples: Stability is key to long-term growth. | The company values financial stability.
context: Why a company should not overspend on buildings

my_sentence: You will be bankrupt in the future.
correction: You will go bankrupt in the future.
explanation: The natural collocation for becoming insolvent is 'go bankrupt' (the change of state). 'Be bankrupt' only describes the static condition.
correct_examples: If they overspend, they will go bankrupt. | The company went bankrupt last year.
context: Risk of a business spending too much

my_sentence: You spent a billion yuan to build your headquarter.
correction: You spent a billion yuan to build your headquarters.
explanation: 'Headquarters' always ends in -s, even when it refers to a single main office. There is no singular form 'headquarter'.
correct_examples: The company built its new headquarters downtown. | Their headquarters is in Beijing.
context: Cost of building a company's main office

my_sentence: The government thinks you are not value to educate.
correction: The government thinks you are not valuable to train or educate.
explanation: After 'are not' you need the adjective 'valuable' (worth investing in), not the noun 'value'.
correct_examples: Older recruits may not be valuable to train. | Her experience makes her valuable to the team.
context: Why people over 40 can't enter the civil service

my_sentence: You will lose opportunity to join.
correction: You will lose the opportunity to join.
explanation: 'Opportunity' is countable here and needs an article: 'the opportunity' or 'an opportunity'. Don't drop the article before a singular countable noun.
correct_examples: Don't miss the opportunity to apply. | He lost a great opportunity to study abroad.
context: Failing the civil-service exam

my_sentence: Finally you can become higher official.
correction: Finally you can become a higher official.
explanation: A singular countable role like 'official' needs an article: 'a higher official'. Don't omit 'a' before a job/position noun.
correct_examples: She became a senior official after years of service. | He wants to be a manager someday.
context: Slowly rising through government ranks

my_sentence: You can't be responsibility for your power.
correction: You can't be responsible for your power.
explanation: After 'be' you need the adjective 'responsible', not the noun 'responsibility'. 'Be responsible for' is the fixed pattern.
correct_examples: You must be responsible for your decisions. | He is responsible for the whole department.
context: Why government leaders need experience

my_sentence: Taxation was very complexity.
correction: Taxation is very complex.
explanation: After 'is/was very' you need the adjective 'complex', not the noun 'complexity'. (And taxation is generally true, so present tense 'is' fits better.)
correct_examples: The tax system is very complex. | This problem is complex and hard to solve.
context: Difficulty of calculating taxes

my_sentence: Each city had their own airport.
correction: Each city has its own airport.
explanation: 'Each city' is singular, so use the singular verb 'has' and the singular possessive 'its'. Also use present tense for a general fact.
correct_examples: Each city has its own airport. | Every region has its own dialect.
context: Airports across Chinese cities

my_sentence: They can connect each together.
correction: They can connect to each other.
explanation: The natural phrase for mutual linking is 'connect to each other' (or 'be connected to each other'). 'Connect each together' is not idiomatic English.
correct_examples: The cities are connected to each other by air. | These two systems connect to each other easily.
context: How city airports link up

my_sentence: The government invests airport.
correction: The government invests in airports.
explanation: Use 'invest in something'. The verb 'invest' needs the preposition 'in' before what receives the money.
correct_examples: They invest in infrastructure. | She invested in real estate years ago.
context: How airports are funded

my_sentence: The land belong to the government.
correction: The land belongs to the government.
explanation: 'The land' is a singular subject, so the present-tense verb takes -s: 'belongs'.
correct_examples: The land belongs to the state. | This building belongs to a private company.
context: Who owns the land under airports

my_sentence: I don't have a friend who works in an airport company.
correction: I don't have a friend who works in an airline company.
explanation: An 'airline' is the company that operates the flights; an 'airport' is the place where planes take off and land. For a company employee, use 'airline'.
correct_examples: She works for an airline. | The airport handles flights from many airlines.
context: Whether Jack knows anyone in aviation

my_sentence: Highways, high-speed rail and public hospitals, they are belong to the government.
correction: Highways, high-speed rail and public hospitals belong to the government.
explanation: 'Belong' is already a full verb, so don't add 'are' before it. Use either 'belong to' or 'are owned by', not 'are belong to'.
correct_examples: These roads belong to the government. | The parks are owned by the city.
context: Listing public assets

my_sentence: Millions of people who bought house from him got nothing.
correction: Millions of people who bought houses from him got nothing.
explanation: 'House' is countable; with many buyers each buying one, use the plural 'houses'. A singular countable noun also can't stand alone without an article.
correct_examples: They bought houses they never received. | He sold apartments to thousands of buyers.
context: A bankrupt real-estate developer

my_sentence: He will locked up in prison for his whole life.
correction: He will be locked up in prison for his whole life.
explanation: This is passive voice, which needs 'be': 'will be locked up'. Don't drop the auxiliary 'be' between the modal and the past participle.
correct_examples: He will be locked up for years. | The files will be reviewed tomorrow.
context: Predicting the developer's fate

my_sentence: They need to invest more than hundred billion yuan every year.
correction: They need to invest more than a hundred billion yuan every year.
explanation: Use 'a hundred' (or 'one hundred') before the number word. English needs the article 'a' before 'hundred/thousand/million' when used as a round figure.
correct_examples: It cost more than a hundred million dollars. | She earns over a thousand a week.
context: How much big firms invest in real estate

my_sentence: No one to buy it, you need to reduce the price.
correction: No one wants to buy it, so you need to reduce the price.
explanation: A clause needs a finite main verb. 'No one to buy it' is missing the verb; say 'no one wants to buy it'.
correct_examples: No one wants to buy the old units. | Nobody wanted to invest in it.
context: When apartments don't sell

my_sentence: Most of them invest some bad companies.
correction: Most of them invest in some bad companies.
explanation: Use 'invest in something'. The preposition 'in' is required before the company or asset.
correct_examples: They invested in the wrong companies. | He put money into a failing startup.
context: Why ordinary people lose money on stocks

my_sentence: The stock market it for the companies to get money, not for ordinary people to share profits.
correction: The stock market is for companies to raise money, not for ordinary people to share profits.
explanation: Don't repeat the subject with a pronoun ('the stock market it...') and don't drop the verb. The clause needs 'is': 'The stock market is for...'.
correct_examples: The market is for raising capital. | This fund is for long-term investors, not gamblers.
context: Jack's view of how the domestic stock market works

my_sentence: I have never tried buy some stocks.
correction: I have never tried buying stocks. / I have never bought stocks.
explanation: After 'try' use the gerund ('-ing') when it means to experiment with doing something: 'try buying'. Don't follow 'try' with a bare infinitive.
correct_examples: I tried buying it online. | Have you ever tried investing?
context: Whether Jack has invested in stocks

my_sentence: You have to apply for bankrupt.
correction: You have to apply for bankruptcy.
explanation: After 'apply for' you need the noun 'bankruptcy'. 'Bankrupt' is an adjective ('go bankrupt', 'be bankrupt'), not a noun.
correct_examples: The firm applied for bankruptcy. | They filed for bankruptcy last month.
context: What a company does when it runs out of money

my_sentence: When I eat with them or have a meeting, I can see check their stock prices.
correction: When I eat with them or have a meeting, I can see them checking their stocks.
explanation: After a perception verb like 'see', use 'see + someone + -ing': 'see them checking'. Don't put two bare verbs ('see check') together.
correct_examples: I saw him checking his phone. | She watched them leaving the office.
context: Coworkers who follow the stock market

my_sentence: It's not the different things.
correction: They are different things.
explanation: You are referring to two plural things (lottery and stocks), so use 'they are', not the singular 'it's'. Also drop 'the' since you mean different things in general.
correct_examples: They are two different things. | Those are completely different problems.
context: Comparing stocks to a lottery ticket

my_sentence: I don't care about entertainment industry.
correction: I don't care about the entertainment industry.
explanation: Use 'the' before a specific, known industry: 'the entertainment industry'. Named industries take the definite article.
correct_examples: He works in the film industry. | The tech industry is booming.
context: Jack's lack of interest in entertainment

my_sentence: The government review any of the movies.
correction: The government reviews many of the movies.
explanation: 'The government' is singular, so the present-tense verb takes -s: 'reviews'. (Here 'many' also fits the meaning better than 'any'.)
correct_examples: The government reviews every film before release. | The agency reviews many applications each year.
context: Film censorship

my_sentence: Some movies for children, some movies for adult.
correction: Some movies are for children and others are for adults.
explanation: 'Adult' is countable, so use the plural 'adults' to match 'children'. Adding the verb 'are' also makes it a complete sentence.
correct_examples: Some shows are for kids and others are for adults. | These films are made for adults only.
context: Movie age classification

my_sentence: It had much violence scene in it.
correction: There were many violent scenes in it.
explanation: Use the adjective 'violent' (not the noun 'violence') before 'scenes', make 'scenes' plural, and use 'there were many' for countable plurals.
correct_examples: There were many violent scenes in the film. | The movie had several scary scenes.
context: Why the Joker movie was banned

my_sentence: A kubernetes cluster is like a group of machine. If someone have problems, it will affect your application.
correction: A kubernetes cluster is like a group of machines. If one of them has a problem, it can affect your application.
explanation: After 'a group of' the noun must be plural ('machines'). Also, a singular subject ('one' / 'someone') takes the -s form of the verb: 'has', not 'have'.
correct_examples: A cluster is a group of machines working together. | If one node has a problem, the others keep running.
context: Explaining what a Kubernetes cluster is

my_sentence: If some of them had some problem, they not will affect the application.
correction: If some of them have a problem, they will not affect the application.
explanation: In a negative statement the word 'not' goes after the auxiliary/modal verb: 'will not', not 'not will'.
correct_examples: They will not affect the service. | It will not change the result.
context: Explaining redundancy across machines in a cluster

my_sentence: Every day there are many customers go to these stores.
correction: Every day there are many customers who go to the stores.
explanation: You cannot put two finite verbs ('are' and 'go') side by side with the same subject. Use a relative clause ('customers who go') to connect them.
correct_examples: There are many people who visit the store every day. | We have customers who shop here daily.
context: Describing how many customers the company's stores serve

my_sentence: When I come back, I didn't find anyone in the home.
correction: When I came back, I didn't find anyone at home.
explanation: The two clauses describe the same past event, so 'come' must be in the past tense ('came') to agree with 'didn't'.
correct_examples: When I came back, the house was empty. | When I got home, nobody was there.
context: Practising adverbial clauses with 'when' to express a complaint

my_sentence: I didn't find anyone in the home.
correction: I didn't find anyone at home.
explanation: The fixed expression is 'at home' (meaning at one's house), not 'in the home'. 'The home' sounds institutional and is not used here.
correct_examples: Nobody was at home. | I stayed at home all weekend.
context: Practising adverbial clauses with 'when' to express a complaint

my_sentence: Mr. John said he wanted to resign from our company and he could not stand the silly customers.
correction: Mr. John said he wanted to resign from our company because he could not stand the silly customers.
explanation: The second clause gives the reason for the first, so use 'because' (cause/effect), not 'and' (which simply adds two separate ideas).
correct_examples: He quit because the work was too stressful. | She left early because she felt sick.
context: Writing a complaint sentence about an employee resigning

my_sentence: Dealing with a difficult customer is the stress for part of the job, so some people want to resign.
correction: Dealing with difficult customers is hard, so a lot of employees are resigning from our company.
explanation: 'Is the stress for part of the job' is not idiomatic. Use a simple adjective ('is hard' / 'is stressful'). Also prefer the general plural 'difficult customers'.
correct_examples: Dealing with difficult customers is hard. | Handling complaints all day is stressful.
context: Giving an opinion about a job using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: Playing basketball is very popular, so many children to join this kind of match.
correction: Playing basketball is very popular, so many children like to join the matches of this sport.
explanation: A clause needs a finite verb: 'many children' must be followed by a verb such as 'like' before the infinitive 'to join'. You cannot jump straight from the noun to 'to join'.
correct_examples: Many children like to join the matches. | A lot of students want to take part in the games.
context: Giving an opinion about playing basketball using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: Many children are like to join this match.
correction: Many children like to join the matches.
explanation: Don't add the auxiliary 'are' before a plain present-tense verb. With 'many children' (plural subject) use the base form directly: 'like', not 'are like'.
correct_examples: Many children like sports. | Most people enjoy weekends.
context: Giving an opinion about playing basketball using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: I can have enough time to prepare other things for other things, such as learning English or play with my daughter.
correction: I can have enough time to prepare for other things, such as learning English or playing with my daughter.
explanation: 'Prepare other things for other things' is redundant; say 'prepare for other things' once. Also keep the list parallel: 'learning' and 'playing' should both be gerunds. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I have time to prepare for other things, such as reading and exercising. | She spends evenings cooking and playing with her kids.
context: Explaining why he eats dinner early

my_sentence: Working from home can improve the efficiency, so more and more people apply from it.
correction: Working from home can improve efficiency, so more and more people apply for it.
explanation: The verb 'apply' takes the preposition 'for' when you mean to request something: 'apply for a job/it', not 'apply from it'.
correct_examples: She applied for the position last week. | Many people apply for remote work these days.
context: Giving an opinion about working from home using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: At first I thought they will speak English. However, they speak Japanese so I can't understand all.
correction: At first I thought they would speak English. However, they spoke Japanese, so I couldn't understand it.
explanation: When the main clause is in the past ('I thought'), the verbs that follow shift back too: 'will' becomes 'would', and present 'speak / can't' become past 'spoke / couldn't'. Keep the whole sentence in the past when narrating a past event.
correct_examples: I thought it would be sunny, but it rained, so I couldn't go out. | She said she would call, but she never did.
context: Telling the teacher about joining an AI meeting that turned out to be in Japanese

my_sentence: I don't forget. I think I don't forget the uses of a comma.
correction: I didn't forget the uses of a comma.
explanation: The teacher was asking about a past action (whether you forgot earlier), so the past tense 'didn't forget' is needed, not the present 'don't forget'.
correct_examples: I didn't forget your birthday. | Don't worry, I didn't forget to lock the door.
context: Teacher asking why there was a comma in the sentence

my_sentence: When I came to home, I complain.
correction: When I come home, I complain.
explanation: 'Home' used as a destination is an adverb, so it takes no preposition: say 'come home' / 'go home', never 'come to home'. (Also watch the tense: 'come' for a habitual present, 'came' only for a past event.)
correct_examples: When I come home, I usually cook dinner. | He went home early yesterday.
context: Practising complaints starting with 'when'

my_sentence: It's very tired for me to finish this project by myself.
correction: It's very tiring for me to finish this project by myself.
explanation: Use the '-ing' adjective for what causes the feeling and the '-ed' adjective for the person who feels it. The project causes the feeling, so it is 'tiring'; you are 'tired'.
correct_examples: The trip was tiring, so I was tired afterwards. | The lecture was boring and I felt bored.
context: Practising complaints

my_sentence: If I can choose, I like to stay at home to finish it.
correction: If I could choose, I would like to stay at home to finish them.
explanation: For a hypothetical situation, use the second conditional: 'if + past tense' in the condition and 'would + verb' in the result ('If I could choose, I would like ...').
correct_examples: If I could choose, I would work from home. | If I had more time, I would learn the piano.
context: Complaining about having to stay in the office

my_sentence: We had to stay in the office to finish our tasks. That makes me feel terrible.
correction: We had to stay in the office to finish our tasks. That made me feel terrible.
explanation: When the story is set in the past ('had to stay'), the following verb must also be past: 'that made me feel', not 'that makes me feel'. This recurred today (e.g. 'that make me feel impatient' -> 'that made me feel impatient').
correct_examples: I missed the bus, and that made me late. | They cancelled the trip, which made everyone upset.
context: Complaining about working overtime in the office

my_sentence: I lost my wallet when I rode the bus to go home, so I hated the thief steal my wallet.
correction: I lost my wallet when I rode the bus home, so I hated the thief who had stolen it.
explanation: You need a relative pronoun ('who') plus a finite verb to attach the clause to 'the thief', and the action happened before you hated him, so use the past perfect 'had stolen'. Don't repeat 'my wallet'; replace it with the pronoun 'it'.
correct_examples: I thanked the man who had found my keys. | She blamed the driver who had hit her car.
context: Expressing resentment about losing a wallet on the bus

my_sentence: I attended an appointment with my coworker, I felt so angry. That's because he was late.
correction: I attended an appointment with my coworker last week and I was so angry towards him because he arrived late and he didn't inform me about it.
explanation: Don't leave a clause hanging and start a new fragment with 'That's because'. Join the ideas into one sentence with conjunctions ('and', 'because') so the reason connects directly to the main clause.
correct_examples: I was angry because he arrived late and didn't tell me why. | I felt tired, so I went to bed early.
context: Complaining about a coworker who was late to a meeting

my_sentence: He arrived late and he didn't give me any message.
correction: He arrived late and he didn't inform me about it.
explanation: 'Inform me about it' (or 'let me know') is the natural way to say someone failed to tell you in advance; 'give me any message' is L1-influenced and sounds unnatural here.
correct_examples: Please inform me about any changes to the schedule. | He left without letting me know.
context: Complaining about a coworker who was late to a meeting

my_sentence: Doing repetitive tasks every day is boring, so it makes us feel despair.
correction: Doing repetitive tasks every day is boring, so it makes us feel desperate.
explanation: After 'feel' you need an adjective. 'Despair' is a noun, so use the adjective 'desperate' ('feel desperate'). You'd only say 'feel despair' as 'feel a sense of despair'.
correct_examples: The endless work made us feel desperate. | She felt hopeless about the situation.
context: Giving an opinion about repetitive jobs using gerund phrases

my_sentence: Being a pilot, it is dangerous.
correction: Being a pilot is dangerous.
explanation: When a gerund phrase ('Being a pilot') is the subject, don't add a second subject pronoun 'it' afterwards. The gerund phrase already is the subject. This recurred (e.g. 'Being a doctor, it's not good' -> 'Being a doctor is not good').
correct_examples: Being a pilot is stressful because it requires a lot of focus. | Learning a language takes time.
context: Giving opinions about jobs using gerund phrases

my_sentence: It can help us to fix many problems.
correction: It can help us fix many problems.
explanation: The causative-style verbs 'help', 'let', and 'make' are followed by a bare infinitive (no 'to'): 'help us fix', 'let you do', 'make me feel'. Today both 'help us to fix' and 'let you to do' had the extra 'to'.
correct_examples: It helps us solve problems faster. | My boss lets me work from home.
context: Giving an opinion about being a computer engineer

my_sentence: Working as a computer engineer is awesome. It allowed you to solve some technical problems.
correction: Working as a computer engineer is awesome. It allows you to solve some technical problems.
explanation: When you state a general opinion in the present tense, keep every verb in the present: 'it allows', not 'it allowed'. Don't switch to the past for a fact that is still true.
correct_examples: This job is great because it allows you to travel. | Exercise helps you stay healthy.
context: Giving an opinion about being a computer engineer

my_sentence: As a scientist, you can use your abilities to help human.
correction: As a scientist, you can use your abilities to help humans.
explanation: To talk about people in general, use the plural 'humans' (or 'people'). Singular 'human' needs an article ('a human'), which changes the meaning to one person.
correct_examples: Scientists work to help humans live longer. | Robots are built to help people with daily tasks.
context: Giving an opinion about being a scientist

my_sentence: A person can earn money by him or her.
correction: A person can earn money by himself or herself.
explanation: After 'by' meaning 'alone / without help', use the reflexive pronoun 'himself / herself', not the object pronoun 'him / her'.
correct_examples: She finished the project by herself. | He can do it by himself.
context: Discussing the pros of having a job

my_sentence: We can use our time to help other fix their problems.
correction: We can use our time to help others fix their problems.
explanation: As a pronoun meaning 'other people', use the plural 'others'. 'Other' on its own is a determiner that needs a noun after it ('other people').
correct_examples: We should help others when we can. | Some people stayed; others left early.
context: Discussing how having a job helps society

my_sentence: Choosing to be a doctor is not a good career that takes a lot of time.
correction: Choosing to be a doctor is not a good career because it takes a lot of time and you can't earn enough money.
explanation: Don't drop a relative 'that' into the middle of a complete clause; it pulls the focus onto the relative clause and away from your main point. Use a conjunction like 'because' to add the reason instead. (assistant note: the teacher flagged the mid-sentence 'that' but the final wording is reconstructed.)
correct_examples: It isn't a good career because it pays little. | I dropped the class because it took too much time.
context: Giving an opinion about being a doctor using gerund phrases

my_sentence: Most of my coworkers will have a rest, so we are rest.
correction: Most of my coworkers will rest, so we'll rest.
explanation: "Rest" is the verb here, so it can't follow "are" as if it were a noun/adjective. Use the verb directly: "we'll rest" (or "we'll take a rest"). "We are rest" is not a valid structure.
correct_examples: My coworkers will rest tomorrow. | We'll take a break this afternoon.
context: Talking about a Japanese holiday and most coworkers resting

my_sentence: Many people need to find a job to consume their energy.
correction: Many people need to find jobs to consume their energy.
explanation: When making a general statement about people in general, use the bare plural ("jobs"), not the singular "a job". The plural matches the plural subject "many people" and expresses the generic idea.
correct_examples: People look for jobs that match their skills. | Students need part-time jobs to earn money.
context: Writing about the importance / pros of jobs

my_sentence: They can use their abilities to provide services and make some contribution for the society.
correction: They can use their abilities to provide services and contribute to society.
explanation: Two issues: use the verb "contribute" rather than "make a contribution" here, and the collocation is "contribute TO" (not "for"). Also "society" as a general concept usually takes no article.
correct_examples: Volunteers contribute to society in many ways. | Everyone can contribute to the community.
context: Explaining how jobs benefit society

my_sentence: It's very useful to help society to progress.
correction: It can be useful to help society progress.
explanation: After "help + object", use the bare infinitive (no "to"): "help society progress", not "help society to progress".
correct_examples: This tool helps students learn faster. | The new policy helped the company grow.
context: Explaining how jobs help society develop

my_sentence: I ordered a delivery to send some fruits to my another apartment.
correction: I ordered a delivery to send some fruit to my other apartment. / to another apartment.
explanation: "Another" cannot follow a possessive like "my". Say either "my other apartment" or "another apartment", not "my another apartment".
correct_examples: I left my keys in the other room. | Let's move to another table.
context: Ordering a delivery to take fruit to a second apartment

my_sentence: I don't have time to send by myself, so I ask someone for help.
correction: I don't have time to send it myself, so I asked someone for help.
explanation: A transitive verb like "send" needs an object: "send it". "Send by myself" drops the thing being sent. "Myself" alone (without "by") is enough to mean 'on my own'.
correct_examples: I can deliver it myself. | She fixed it herself.
context: Why he used a delivery service instead of going himself

my_sentence: This can reduce several criminal events.
correction: This can reduce several criminal activities.
explanation: Use "criminal activities", not "criminal events". "Events" are things that happen/occur; "activities" are the actions or doings people carry out, which is what crime refers to.
correct_examples: The police cracked down on criminal activities. | These programs reduce gang activity in the area.
context: Reading his written sentences about jobs reducing crime

my_sentence: I ordered a delivery. Its delivery is so quick, just 10 minutes.
correction: I ordered a delivery. It was so quick, just 10 minutes.
explanation: Since the delivery already happened (past event), describe it in the past tense: "it was so quick", not the present "is".
correct_examples: The food arrived fast; it was so quick. | The trip was great, only two hours.
context: Describing how fast the delivery service was

my_sentence: I haven't experienced it, but many people had have experienced it.
correction: I haven't experienced it, but many people have. / but many people did.
explanation: Don't stack auxiliaries ("had have experienced"). After the contrast, just use the bare auxiliary instead of repeating the whole verb: "...but many people have" (or "...but many people did").
correct_examples: I haven't seen it, but my friends have. | He didn't finish, but the others did.
context: Talking about the eye-twitch reflex

my_sentence: You can use Grok to search information very quickly and convenient.
correction: You can use Grok to search for information very quickly and conveniently.
explanation: Modifying a verb ("search") requires an adverb, so coordinate two adverbs: "quickly and conveniently", not the adjective "convenient".
correct_examples: The app works quickly and smoothly. | She explained it clearly and patiently.
context: Comparing AI tools like Grok

my_sentence: You will find a lot of problem in it, and for some question it can't understand.
correction: You will find a lot of problems in it, and there are some questions it can't understand.
explanation: Countable nouns after "a lot of" and "some" must be plural: "a lot of problems", "some questions".
correct_examples: There are a lot of bugs in this code. | I have some questions about the plan.
context: Saying Grok's code output has many problems

my_sentence: Many years ago I used it, but now I didn't use it.
correction: Many years ago I used it, but now I'm not using it anymore.
explanation: For a current situation, use the present ("I'm not using it anymore" / "I don't use it anymore"), not the past "I didn't use it". Past tense would refer to a finished past time, which contradicts "now".
correct_examples: I used to play tennis, but now I don't. | I had a car before, but I'm not driving anymore.
context: Talking about no longer using Netflix

my_sentence: That's because the Japan company, they don't trust.
correction: That's because Japanese companies don't trust them.
explanation: Use the adjective form "Japanese" to describe companies, not the country noun "Japan": "Japanese companies". Also avoid the redundant double subject ("the company, they...") — just "Japanese companies don't trust...".
correct_examples: Japanese companies value teamwork. | Many Chinese companies export to Europe.
context: Why his company uses Google/Anthropic models instead of Chinese ones

my_sentence: 20 years ago in my country, there are many people lose their job.
correction: 20 years ago in my country, there were many people who lost their jobs.
explanation: A past time marker ("20 years ago") needs past tense: "there were" and "lost". Also, to attach the action to "people", use a relative clause: "people who lost their jobs", and the plural "jobs".
correct_examples: There were many workers who lost their jobs during the crisis. | Years ago, many people moved to the cities.
context: Example of unemployment leading to crime

my_sentence: If someone finish his or her work, he or she will be very tired.
correction: If someone finishes his or her work, he or she will be very tired.
explanation: Third-person singular subjects ("someone", "he", "she") take the -s form of the verb in the present: "finishes".
correct_examples: When he finishes work, he goes home. | If someone needs help, she calls me.
context: Saying busy people are too tired to commit crimes

my_sentence: They will feel so tired, tiring and exhausting.
correction: They will feel so tired and exhausted.
explanation: Use the -ed participle for how a person feels ("tired", "exhausted"); the -ing form ("tiring", "exhausting") describes what causes the feeling. A person is exhausted; the work is exhausting.
correct_examples: After the trip I was exhausted. | The kids were tired after playing all day.
context: Describing how people feel after finishing work

my_sentence: If you did a bad thing, you will be catch quickly.
correction: If you do a bad thing, you will be caught quickly.
explanation: The passive needs the past participle: "be caught", not the base form "catch". ("Catch-caught-caught".)
correct_examples: Criminals are usually caught on camera. | He was caught cheating on the test.
context: Saying CCTV cameras mean criminals get caught fast

my_sentence: If you do bad thing, you will be caught.
correction: If you do a bad thing, you will be caught.
explanation: A singular countable noun like "thing" needs an article: "a bad thing".
correct_examples: If you make a mistake, fix it quickly. | He did a good deed today.
context: Talking about CCTV deterring crime

my_sentence: For an apartment, maybe hundreds of cameras in it.
correction: For an apartment, there would be hundreds of cameras in it.
explanation: An English clause needs a subject and verb. To state that something exists, use "there is / there are / there would be": "there would be hundreds of cameras", not just "hundreds of cameras in it".
correct_examples: There are many shops in this mall. | There would be a lot of people at the event.
context: Estimating how many CCTV cameras an apartment complex has

my_sentence: That's because there are too many CCTV camera company.
correction: That's because there are too many CCTV camera companies.
explanation: After "too many", the countable noun must be plural: "companies".
correct_examples: There are too many cars on the road. | She has too many books to read.
context: Explaining why CCTV cameras are cheap in China

my_sentence: I have a good day today.
correction: I had a good day today.
explanation: When you talk about a day that is essentially over (your class is at night), use the past simple. 'I have a good day' describes a present/general state; the completed day takes 'had'.
correct_examples: I had a good day today. | I had a busy day at work yesterday.
context: Small talk at the start of class.

my_sentence: Today was the holiday for Japanese members, so I can do some studying for myself.
correction: Today was a holiday for the Japanese members, so I was able to do some studying for myself.
explanation: For an ability that was realized on a specific past occasion, use 'was/were able to', not the present 'can'. 'Can' here clashes with the past-tense clause before it.
correct_examples: The office was closed, so I was able to finish my own project. | It was a holiday, so I was able to rest.
context: Explaining a holiday gave him free time to study.

my_sentence: Talked something with my coworker.
correction: Talked about something with my coworkers.
explanation: 'Talk' takes 'about' before the topic: 'talk about something'. Also, since there was more than one colleague, use the plural 'coworkers'.
correct_examples: I talked about the project with my coworkers. | We talked about the news over lunch.
context: Describing his day at work.

my_sentence: I eat my dinner. I eat something.
correction: I ate my dinner. / I ate some.
explanation: Dinner is already finished, so use the past simple 'ate'. You repeatedly slipped between 'eat' and 'ate' — anchor completed actions in the past tense.
correct_examples: I ate dinner an hour ago. | I ate some vegetables and fruit.
context: Saying what he had for dinner.

my_sentence: Their families will go bankrupt at the end.
correction: Their families will go bankrupt in the end.
explanation: 'In the end' means 'finally / as the final outcome'. 'At the end' needs an object ('at the end of the month') and refers to a point in time/space, not a final result.
correct_examples: In the end, they lost everything. | At the end of the movie, the hero wins.
context: Describing how gambling ruins families.

my_sentence: One day he addicted to gambling.
correction: One day he got addicted to gambling.
explanation: 'Addicted' is a past participle/adjective, not a verb on its own. Use 'get/become addicted to' for the change, or 'be addicted to' for the state. You made this slip several times today.
correct_examples: He got addicted to gambling. | She is addicted to her phone.
context: Telling the story of a coworker's husband.

my_sentence: He ran out his money.
correction: He ran out of his money.
explanation: The phrasal verb is 'run out of (something)'. The 'of' is required before the object.
correct_examples: He ran out of money. | We ran out of time before finishing.
context: The husband used up all his money gambling.

my_sentence: My coworker had to divorced with him.
correction: My coworker had to divorce him.
explanation: After 'had to' use the base verb ('divorce'), not the past form. Also 'divorce' is transitive — you divorce someone, with no 'with' (assistant note: same goes for 'marry someone').
correct_examples: She had to divorce him. | They divorced last year.
context: The wife divorced her gambling husband.

my_sentence: Her husband, he need to return by himself.
correction: Her husband needs to return it by himself.
explanation: Third-person singular subjects take '-s': 'he needs', not 'he need'. This was a recurring error throughout today's class.
correct_examples: He needs to repay the money himself. | She needs to leave early today.
context: Who is responsible for repaying the debt.

my_sentence: If you kill someone, you will be killed by the government.
correction: If you kill someone, you will be punished by the law.
explanation: Say 'punished by the law' for legal consequences. 'Killed by the government' is too literal and unidiomatic; the law (not the government as an actor) punishes crimes.
correct_examples: Anyone who breaks the law will be punished by the law. | He was punished by the law for fraud.
context: Discussing the death penalty.

my_sentence: There are many companies will harass him every day.
correction: There are many companies that will harass him every day.
explanation: A subject relative clause needs a relative pronoun ('that'/'which'/'who') to link it to the noun. Without 'that', you have two verbs ('are', 'will harass') with no connector.
correct_examples: There are many companies that lend money. | I know a person who can help you.
context: Debt-collection companies harassing the debtor.

my_sentence: If someone don't return your money, you can report to the police.
correction: If someone doesn't return your money, you can report it to the police.
explanation: 'Someone' is third-person singular, so use 'doesn't', not 'don't'. (Also add the object 'it' after 'report'.)
correct_examples: If someone doesn't pay, you can take legal action. | If someone doesn't reply, send a reminder.
context: What to do when a debtor won't repay.

my_sentence: Even if you win the case, you couldn't get your money anymore.
correction: Even if you win the case, you would not be able to get your money back.
explanation: In a hypothetical outcome use 'would not be able to' rather than 'couldn't ... anymore'. 'Be able to' also reads more naturally than bare 'could' for future possibility, and 'get ... back' is the natural phrasing for recovering money.
correct_examples: Even if you win, you would not be able to recover the money. | You would not be able to get it back.
context: Winning a lawsuit but the debtor has nothing.

my_sentence: I don't think we need to depend on one or two person to protect us.
correction: I don't think we need to depend on one or two people to protect us.
explanation: After a number greater than one, use the plural 'people' (the plural of 'person').
correct_examples: We can't depend on one or two people. | Two people were waiting outside.
context: Arguing that society should rely on law, not individuals.

my_sentence: People will change time by time.
correction: People change from time to time.
explanation: The fixed idiom is 'from time to time' (= occasionally). 'Time by time' is not an English expression.
correct_examples: People change from time to time. | From time to time, he visits his hometown.
context: Arguing that leaders can change over time.

my_sentence: Even though the law is not perfect, however, at least it can protect most ordinary people.
correction: Even though the law is not perfect, it can at least protect most ordinary people.
explanation: (assistant note) Don't pair 'even though' with 'however' — they both signal contrast, so using both is redundant. Use one: 'Even though X, Y' OR 'X; however, Y'. You combine them often.
correct_examples: Even though the law is not perfect, it still protects ordinary people. | The law is not perfect; however, it protects ordinary people.
context: Defending the rule of law.

my_sentence: The major need to report for the President.
correction: The mayor needs to report to the President.
explanation: You 'report to' a person or authority, not 'report for'. (Also third-person 'needs', and the word is 'mayor' for a city leader.)
correct_examples: The manager reports to the director. | Each governor reports to the central government.
context: Explaining the chain of command in government.

my_sentence: It started when 1950.
correction: It started in 1950.
explanation: Use 'in' before a year. 'When' introduces a clause (when + subject + verb), not a bare year.
correct_examples: The company started in 1950. | It began in 2008, when the crisis hit.
context: Describing when the government system began.

my_sentence: It started in 1950 when the country built.
correction: It started in 1950, when the country was built.
explanation: The country doesn't build itself — it 'was built', so use the passive 'was/got built'. Without 'was', the sentence reads as if the country actively built something.
correct_examples: The system began when the country was built. | The bridge was built in 1950.
context: The founding of the country.

my_sentence: It did not affect nowadays.
correction: It did not affect us nowadays.
explanation: 'Affect' is transitive and needs an object. Add 'us' (or 'our lives') — you can't 'affect' with nothing being affected.
correct_examples: It did not affect us at the time. | The change didn't affect our daily lives.
context: Saying current policies won't affect people until the future.

my_sentence: It will affect for the future.
correction: It will affect the future.
explanation: 'Affect' takes a direct object with no preposition. Don't insert 'for' (or 'to') after it.
correct_examples: It will affect the future. | This decision will affect our children.
context: Long-term effects of current leadership.

my_sentence: Our current life originated from the leaders of ten years ago.
correction: Our current lives originated from the leaders of ten years ago.
explanation: When 'we/our' refers to many people, use the plural 'lives' (pronounced /laɪvz/), not 'life'. You repeated 'our life nowadays' several times.
correct_examples: Our lives have changed a lot. | Our current lives depend on past decisions.
context: Today's prosperity comes from past leaders.

my_sentence: If you have your own company, you can't affair government.
correction: If you have your own company, you can't oversee the affairs of the government.
explanation: 'Affair' is a noun, not a verb. To manage/be in charge of something, use a verb like 'oversee' or 'handle': 'oversee government affairs'.
correct_examples: Civil servants oversee government affairs. | She handles the affairs of the company.
context: Separation between business and government roles.

my_sentence: You can go any countries.
correction: You can go to any country.
explanation: 'Go' needs 'to' before a destination, and 'any' here takes the singular 'country' ('any country' = whichever one).
correct_examples: With this passport you can go to any country. | You can travel to any country you like.
context: What a diplomatic passport allows.

my_sentence: A person who marry with a Chinese citizen can get a green card.
correction: A person who marries a Chinese citizen can get a green card.
explanation: 'Marry' is transitive — you marry someone, with no 'with'. Also, the relative pronoun 'who' refers to a singular person, so use 'marries'.
correct_examples: A person who marries a citizen can apply for residency. | She married a doctor last year.
context: Getting a green card through marriage.

my_sentence: I think maybe more than two hundred millions.
correction: I think maybe more than two hundred million.
explanation: When 'million' / 'billion' follows an exact number (two hundred, five, etc.), it stays singular: 'two hundred million', not 'two hundred millions'. You only add -s in the vague plural form 'millions of ...'.
correct_examples: China alone has about two hundred million mobile game users. | The film earned more than five billion yuan.
context: Estimating the number of mobile game users in China.

my_sentence: They will charge more than hundreds of billion yuan.
correction: They can charge hundreds of billions of yuan.
explanation: In the vague-quantity pattern the noun is plural and takes 'of': 'hundreds of billions of yuan'. Compare with rule for exact numbers ('two hundred million'): after 'hundreds of', use plural 'billions of'.
correct_examples: The industry generates hundreds of billions of yuan every year. | They spend thousands of dollars on games.
context: How much money mobile games generate yearly in China.

my_sentence: Tencent company in my country is the biggest.
correction: Tencent Company is the biggest in my country.
explanation: Keep the subject and its verb together, and put the place phrase after the complement: 'Tencent is the biggest [company] in my country'. Don't split the subject with 'in my country'.
correct_examples: Tencent is the biggest company in my country. | This is the tallest building in the city.
context: Saying which gaming company is the biggest in China.

my_sentence: I don't play computer games, so I don't know something about them.
correction: I don't play computer games, so I don't know anything about them.
explanation: In a negative sentence use 'anything', not 'something'. 'I don't know something' is a Chinese-influenced pattern; the negative polarity word is 'anything'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I don't know anything about it. | She didn't say anything about the plan.
context: Saying he doesn't know the game Gameloft.

my_sentence: You don't want to go to another places just to watch a movie.
correction: You don't want to go to another place just to watch a movie.
