explanation: 'another' is followed by a singular noun ('another place'); for a plural noun use 'other' ('other places'). The teacher stressed: another + singular, other + plural.
correct_examples: Let's try another place next time. | There are many other places worth visiting.
context: Why people prefer watching movies at home rather than travelling to a cinema.

my_sentence: If you want to build a tent, it's not suitable when is raining.
correction: It's not suitable if it's raining.
explanation: A weather clause needs the dummy subject 'it': 'when/if it's raining', not 'when is raining'. English clauses can't drop the subject the way Chinese does.
correct_examples: We can't go out because it's raining. | It's not a good idea to camp when it's raining.
context: Explaining why you can't camp outdoors on a rainy day.

my_sentence: I'm all enjoy the winter day.
correction: I enjoy winter more.
explanation: Don't put 'be' before an ordinary verb: use the plain present 'I enjoy', not 'I'm ... enjoy'. Also, the season is just 'winter' (no 'day'), and for a preference use 'more'.
correct_examples: I enjoy winter more than summer. | I really enjoy reading.
context: Saying which season he likes best.

my_sentence: There are many snow in my city.
correction: There is a lot of snow in my city.
explanation: 'snow' is uncountable, so it takes singular 'is' and the quantifier 'a lot of' / 'much', not 'many'. 'many' only goes with countable plural nouns.
correct_examples: There is a lot of snow in winter. | We don't get much rain here.
context: Explaining why he enjoys winter.

my_sentence: Most of them don't have English name.
correction: Most of them don't have English names.
explanation: When several films each have a name, use the plural 'names'. A countable noun referring to more than one thing should be plural.
correct_examples: These movies don't have English names. | The students wrote their names on the sheet.
context: Chinese animated movies not being known abroad.

my_sentence: I think I didn't heard of it.
correction: I didn't hear of it. / I haven't heard of it.
explanation: After the auxiliary 'did/didn't' the main verb stays in the base form: 'didn't hear', not 'didn't heard'. To stress it's news up to now, 'I haven't heard of it' is even more natural.
correct_examples: I didn't hear about the meeting. | I haven't heard of that movie.
context: Saying he had never heard of the anime 'Legend of Aang'.

my_sentence: There are too much poor people, they don't have money to watch movies.
correction: There are too many poor people, so they don't have money to watch movies.
explanation: 'people' is countable plural, so use 'too many', not 'too much' (which is for uncountable nouns). (assistant note)
correct_examples: There are too many people in this room. | I have too much work today.
context: Explaining why many Indians don't spend money on movies.

my_sentence: Their films are not enough good.
correction: Their films are not good enough.
explanation: 'enough' comes AFTER an adjective: 'good enough', not 'enough good'. (It comes before a noun: 'enough money'.)
correct_examples: This film isn't good enough to win a prize. | The room isn't big enough for everyone.
context: Saying Hong Kong films are no longer good enough.

my_sentence: Nowadays their films become worse and worse.
correction: Nowadays their films are becoming worse and worse.
explanation: For a change happening over time right now, use the present continuous: 'are becoming worse and worse'. The '... and ...' comparative pattern describes an ongoing trend.
correct_examples: The weather is getting colder and colder. | The city is becoming more and more crowded.
context: Describing the decline of Hong Kong cinema.

my_sentence: There are too many companies that built in China.
correction: There are too many companies that are being built in China.
explanation: The companies are built by someone, so this needs the passive: 'that are being built'. 'that built' is active and would mean the companies did the building.
correct_examples: Many new factories are being built in the area. | These houses were built ten years ago.
context: Explaining why Hong Kong studios now face many competitors.

my_sentence: For me, I'm more prefer to earn more money, not become famous.
correction: I prefer to earn more money, not to become famous.
explanation: 'prefer' is a normal verb: say 'I prefer', not 'I'm prefer' (no 'be', no 'more'). Keep the two options parallel with 'to': 'to earn money, not to become famous'.
correct_examples: I prefer to stay home rather than go out. | She chose to study, not to travel.
context: Saying he'd rather make money than be famous.

my_sentence: You need to join private classes to improve, and improve, and improve.
correction: You need to join private classes to make progress.
explanation: Don't repeat 'improve' over and over; vary it with 'make progress'. Note the collocation is 'make progress' (uncountable, no article) — not 'have a progress'.
correct_examples: Extra classes help students make progress. | She has made a lot of progress this year.
context: Talking about private tutoring to raise children's scores.

my_sentence: I think it's very similar from other countries.
correction: I think it's very similar to schools in other countries.
explanation: 'similar' takes 'to', not 'from': 'similar to ...'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: This system is similar to the one in Japan. | Her opinion is similar to mine.
context: Comparing international schools in China with schools abroad.

my_sentence: If their classes have more than 50 students, their tuition fee will very low.
correction: If their classes have more than 50 students, their tuition fee will be very low.
explanation: 'will' must be followed by a base verb; before an adjective you need 'be': 'will be very low'. You can't link a subject to an adjective without a verb.
correct_examples: The price will be very high next year. | If it rains, the road will be slippery.
context: How class size affects tuition fees.

my_sentence: This money need to pay by their parents.
correction: This money needs to be paid by their parents.
explanation: Two fixes: 'money' is singular, so 'needs' (agreement); and the money is paid by someone, so the passive infinitive 'to be paid' is required, not the active 'to pay'.
correct_examples: This bill needs to be paid by Friday. | The form needs to be signed by a parent.
context: Who pays international-school tuition fees.

my_sentence: The government will pay zero for these schools.
correction: The government will not pay any amount of money to these schools.
explanation: 'pay zero for' sounds unnatural. Use 'not pay any money to ...' (or simply 'pays nothing toward these schools'). Also note you 'pay money TO' an institution.
correct_examples: The government doesn't pay any money to private schools. | They paid nothing toward the repairs.
context: Saying the government funds nothing for international schools.

my_sentence: I mentioned that before; I find their tuition fee is so high.
correction: I found that their tuition fee was so high.
explanation: Reporting something you said before takes the past tense, and the reported clause backshifts too: 'I found that their tuition fee was so high.'
correct_examples: I found that the course was very useful. | She said the price was too high.
context: Recalling an earlier comment about high university tuition abroad.

my_sentence: I brought my daughter go to the shopping mall to buy something.
correction: I brought my daughter to the shopping mall.
explanation: 'bring someone somewhere' already carries the motion, so you don't add a second verb 'go to'. Use 'bring/take + person + to + place' directly.
correct_examples: I took my daughter to the shopping mall. | She brought her son to the park.
context: Talking about the weekend with his daughter

my_sentence: We need to send her to hospital to do some check up.
correction: We needed to send her to the hospital to do a check up.
explanation: Use 'the hospital' (a specific place) and 'a check up' (a single countable event). Also, since this was a past situation, the verb should be 'needed', not 'need'.
correct_examples: I took her to the hospital for a check up. | He went to the clinic for a quick check up.
context: Taking his daughter for a health check

my_sentence: There are too many students graduated in one year.
correction: There are too many students who graduate in the same year.
explanation: To modify 'students' with an action, use a relative clause 'who graduate', not the bare past participle 'graduated'. For a general yearly fact, use the present 'graduate', and 'in the same year' for the intended meaning.
correct_examples: There are too many students who graduate in the same year. | Many people who graduate each year cannot find a job.
context: Why college graduates struggle to find jobs

my_sentence: After they graduated from college, they can't find a suitable job.
correction: After they graduate from college, they can't find a suitable job.
explanation: For a general, repeated truth, both clauses stay in the present: 'After they graduate ... they can't find ...'. The past 'graduated' would only fit a specific past event.
correct_examples: After they graduate, many young people move to big cities. | After he finishes school, he looks for work.
context: General pattern of graduates and jobs

my_sentence: That means they have a terrible childhood.
correction: That means they had a terrible childhood.
explanation: Childhood is finished and in the past, so use the past tense 'had'. 'have' would wrongly suggest it is still happening now.
correct_examples: They had a difficult childhood. | She had a happy childhood in the countryside.
context: Children pushed too hard by parents

my_sentence: Children must need to learn 20 to 30 words this day.
correction: Children must learn 20 to 30 words.
explanation: Don't stack two modals/semi-modals. 'must' already means obligation, so 'must need to' is redundant; say 'must learn' or 'need to learn', not both.
correct_examples: Students must learn 20 new words a day. | They need to learn 20 new words a day.
context: Describing a strict parent's demands on a teacher

my_sentence: They get educated from 1978.
correction: They started getting education in 1978.
explanation: To mark the beginning of something at a point in time, use 'started + -ing' and the preposition 'in' for a year: 'started getting education in 1978'. 'get educated from 1978' is ungrammatical here.
correct_examples: Women started getting an education in 1978. | He started learning English in 2010.
context: When women in China began to be educated

my_sentence: Some people want to finish their life and try to suicide.
correction: Some people want to commit suicide.
explanation: 'suicide' is a noun, not a verb, and the fixed collocation is 'commit suicide'. You cannot say 'to suicide' or 'finish their life'.
correct_examples: Sadly, some students commit suicide from the pressure. | He was so depressed he thought about committing suicide.
context: Consequences of excessive academic pressure

my_sentence: We need to reduce some stress.
correction: We need to reduce stress. / We need to reduce being stressed out.
explanation: 'stress' here is uncountable and general, so drop 'some'. Native speakers say 'reduce stress' or, more colloquially, 'reduce being stressed out'.
correct_examples: We need to reduce stress on students. | Exercise helps reduce stress.
context: Lowering pressure on children

my_sentence: They find being an ordinary people is good.
correction: They find being an ordinary person is good.
explanation: 'an' is singular, so it must take the singular noun 'person'. 'people' is plural and cannot follow 'an'.
correct_examples: Being an ordinary person is enough. | He is just an ordinary person.
context: Parents accepting an ordinary life for their children

my_sentence: We need to reduce our expectation.
correction: We need to reduce our expectations.
explanation: 'expectation' is usually plural when talking about hopes in general: 'lower/reduce your expectations'.
correct_examples: We should lower our expectations. | Parents need to manage their expectations.
context: Parents lowering their hopes for their children

my_sentence: It is just to look ourself.
correction: It is just to look out for ourselves.
explanation: The phrasal verb 'look out for' means 'take care of / protect', and the reflexive pronoun must be plural 'ourselves' to match 'we'.
correct_examples: We just have to look out for ourselves. | Look out for yourself first.
context: Being realistic as ordinary people

my_sentence: Why our children need to be an elite?
correction: Why would our children need to become an elite?
explanation: A question needs an auxiliary and subject-auxiliary inversion: 'Why would our children ...?'. Also 'become an elite' fits the idea of turning into one better than 'be'.
correct_examples: Why would our children need to become elites? | Why do they have to be the best?
context: Questioning the pressure to make children elite

my_sentence: They do not think the results.
correction: They do not think about the results.
explanation: 'think' needs the preposition 'about' when it means 'consider': 'think about the results / consequences'.
correct_examples: They don't think about the consequences. | Think about your future.
context: Parents ignoring the consequences of pressure

my_sentence: I eat something before our classes.
correction: I ate something before our class.
explanation: The eating already happened before the class started, so use the past tense 'ate'.
correct_examples: I ate dinner before the meeting. | I had a snack before class.
context: Whether he had eaten before the lesson

my_sentence: We couldn't finish our homework tonight.
correction: We couldn't finish our homework at night.
explanation: 'tonight' means the night of today; for a general habit in the past use 'at night'. He was describing childhood evenings, not this evening.
correct_examples: We studied at night by candlelight. | He often works at night.
context: Childhood with no electricity

my_sentence: We need to do my homework until ten o'clock.
correction: We needed to do our homework until ten o'clock.
explanation: This was a past habit, so use 'needed'. Also keep the pronoun consistent: after 'we', use 'our homework', not 'my homework'.
correct_examples: We needed to do our homework until late at night. | We had to finish our homework by ten.
context: Heavy homework in middle and high school

my_sentence: Some adults can't thinking.
correction: Some adults can't think.
explanation: After a modal verb like 'can', use the bare base form 'think', not the '-ing' form.
correct_examples: Some people can't think for themselves. | He can't make decisions on his own.
context: Adults who only follow mainstream ideas

my_sentence: The government need to spend a lot of money to pay for that.
correction: The government needs to spend a lot of money to pay for it.
explanation: 'The government' is singular, so the verb takes -s: 'needs'. Also use 'pay for it' to refer back to a single thing already mentioned.
correct_examples: The government needs to invest in education. | The company needs to pay for it.
context: Cost of power failures to factories

my_sentence: If a child don't have enough education, he or she can't find a suitable job.
correction: If a child doesn't have enough education, he or she would not be able to find a suitable job.
explanation: 'a child' is singular, so use 'doesn't', not 'don't'. In this hypothetical, 'would not be able to find' is more natural than 'can't find'.
correct_examples: If a child doesn't get a good education, he or she will struggle later. | If a student doesn't study, he won't pass.
context: Consequences of neglecting a child's education

my_sentence: He or she needs to be take care of their parents.
correction: He or she needs to be taken care of by his or her parents.
explanation: In a passive form after 'be', use the past participle 'taken', not 'take'. The agent is introduced with 'by', and keep the pronoun consistent ('his or her', not 'their').
correct_examples: She needs to be taken care of by her family. | The patient must be taken care of by a nurse.
context: An uneducated person depending on family

my_sentence: I never been bullied.
correction: I was never bullied.
explanation: A passive past statement needs the verb 'be': 'I was never bullied'. 'I never been' is missing 'was' (or, with 'have', it would be 'I have never been bullied').
correct_examples: I was never bullied at school. | He has never been bullied.
context: Whether he was ever bullied as a child

my_sentence: The school reputation will go down.
correction: The school's reputation will go down.
explanation: To show that the reputation belongs to the school, use the possessive 's: 'the school's reputation'.
correct_examples: The school's reputation will suffer. | The company's image was damaged.
context: Schools that fail their students

my_sentence: That's a disaster for he or her family, too.
correction: That's a disaster for his or her family, too.
explanation: Before a noun you need the possessive 'his or her', not the subject pronoun 'he'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected, but consistently wrong.)
correct_examples: It's a disaster for his or her family. | Each student should bring his or her book.
context: Effect of a ruined life on the family

my_sentence: They always break the red light.
correction: They always run a red light. / They always run red lights.
explanation: The natural collocation for ignoring a traffic light is 'run a red light', not 'break the red light'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected in class.)
correct_examples: He ran a red light and almost caused an accident. | Don't run red lights.
context: Students who ignore traffic rules

my_sentence: They will have a happy life and pretty good friend.
correction: They can connect with some pretty good friends.
explanation: 'friend' should be plural 'friends', and the natural verb for making friends here is 'connect with', not 'have'.
correct_examples: They can make some good friends. | Kids connect with friends when they have free time.
context: Benefits of less homework for children

my_sentence: If you work for the company many years, you will have some leave days.
correction: If you work for the company for many years, you will have some leave days.
explanation: A duration phrase like 'many years' needs the preposition 'for' before it: 'work for the company for many years'. Without 'for' the time span dangles after the object.
correct_examples: She has lived here for ten years. | If you stay with us for many years, you earn extra leave.
context: Explaining how labor law grants paid leave by years of service

my_sentence: It can give some free time to employees to help them rest.
correction: It can give the employees free time to rest.
explanation: With 'give', the natural double-object order is 'give + recipient + thing' ('give the employees free time'), and the recipient takes a definite article here. 'to help them rest' is wordy; 'to rest' is enough.
correct_examples: The policy gives employees free time to rest. | It gave the staff a chance to recover.
context: Why companies must follow the paid-leave law

my_sentence: If you want to get more salary, you need to reduce your leave days.
correction: If you want to be paid more, you need to reduce your leave days.
explanation: Native speakers say 'be paid more' or 'get a higher salary', not 'get more salary' (salary is not usually counted that way). 'a higher salary rate' is also natural.
correct_examples: If you want to be paid more, take on more responsibility. | Managers get a higher salary but less free time.
context: Trade-off between salary and leave days for managers

my_sentence: If you become the top player, you need to work at Saturdays.
correction: If you become a top executive, you need to work on Saturdays.
explanation: Use 'on' with specific days of the week: 'on Saturdays'. 'at Saturdays' is ungrammatical. ('during the weekends' also works for the general period.)
correct_examples: I usually work on Saturdays. | She volunteers on Sundays.
context: Senior staff having to work weekends

my_sentence: They are provide the same price.
correction: They provide the same price.
explanation: (assistant note) Don't put 'are' before a present-tense main verb. Either 'they provide' (simple present) or 'they are providing' (continuous), never 'they are provide'.
correct_examples: They provide the same service. | These companies offer the same price.
context: Comparing prices of online English companies

my_sentence: They are in the same level, they provide the same price.
correction: They are at the same level; they provide the same price.
explanation: (assistant note) The collocation is 'at the same level', not 'in the same level'. Use 'at' for a point on a scale or rank.
correct_examples: These two players are at the same level. | The courses are pitched at the same level.
context: Comparing online English companies

my_sentence: I mean you will get death penalty.
correction: I mean you will get the death penalty.
explanation: 'death penalty' is a specific, definite punishment, so it takes 'the': 'get the death penalty'. (Same for 'face the death penalty'.)
correct_examples: Drug trafficking can carry the death penalty. | He was sentenced to the death penalty.
context: Strict drug laws in China

my_sentence: They are sent to prison for many years.
correction: They can be sent to prison for many years.
explanation: You were describing a possible consequence, not something that always happens, so use the modal 'can be sent' to express possibility rather than the bare passive 'are sent'.
correct_examples: Offenders can be sent to prison for years. | If caught, they can be fined or jailed.
context: What happens to celebrities addicted to drugs

my_sentence: He addicted to drugs.
correction: He is addicted to drugs.
explanation: 'addicted' is an adjective/past participle here, so it needs the verb 'be': 'He is addicted to drugs'. 'He addicted to drugs' has no finite verb.
correct_examples: He is addicted to gambling. | Many people are addicted to their phones.
context: A banned celebrity addicted to drugs

my_sentence: He was banned by the government because of drug.
correction: He was banned by the government because of drugs.
explanation: When 'drug' refers to illegal substances in general, use the plural 'drugs'. The teacher corrected 'drug' to 'drugs' several times.
correct_examples: He was arrested because of drugs. | She got addicted to drugs.
context: A celebrity banned over drugs

my_sentence: They are not addicted to it.
correction: They are not addicted to them.
explanation: The pronoun must agree with its plural antecedent 'drugs', so use 'them', not 'it'.
correct_examples: These kids aren't addicted to drugs; they aren't even exposed to them. | I don't like those games, so I'm not addicted to them.
context: Whether young offenders get addicted to drugs

my_sentence: Most parents who live in countryside have to stay at countryside.
correction: Most parents who live in the countryside have to stay in the countryside.
explanation: 'countryside' is normally used with 'the': 'in the countryside'. Also use 'in', not 'at', for this area.
correct_examples: They grew up in the countryside. | Life in the countryside is quieter.
context: Rural parents leaving children with grandparents

my_sentence: If I want to travel to Japan, the government ask me to provide some documents.
correction: If I want to travel to Japan, the government asks me to provide some documents.
explanation: 'the government' is third-person singular, so the present-tense verb needs '-s': 'asks', 'needs', 'requires'.
correct_examples: The government requires a visa for that trip. | The embassy asks applicants to show their savings.
context: Visa documents required to travel abroad

my_sentence: The Japan's government needs me to provide some documents.
correction: The Japanese government requires me to provide some documents.
explanation: Use the adjective form of the country ('the Japanese government'), not the possessive 'Japan's government'. 'requires' is also more natural than 'needs' for an official demand.
correct_examples: The Japanese government requires a visa. | The Chinese government issued new rules.
context: Visa requirements for travel to Japan

my_sentence: If there are too many poor people who went to a more developed country, it will cause a disaster.
correction: If there are too many poor people who go to a more developed country, it will cause a disaster.
explanation: In this general/hypothetical 'if' clause about something that could happen, use the present simple 'go', not the past 'went'.
correct_examples: If people who move to a richer country can't find work, problems follow. | Students who go abroad often face culture shock.
context: Why developed countries restrict visas

my_sentence: They only require fix the number, not all.
correction: They only require a fixed number, not all.
explanation: Use the past-participle adjective 'fixed' (a set quantity), with the article 'a': 'a fixed number'. 'fix the number' uses the bare verb where an adjective is needed.
correct_examples: They hire a fixed number of workers each year. | There is a fixed quota for visas.
context: Countries recruiting a set number of foreign workers

my_sentence: The HR department will according to the result.
correction: The HR department will decide based on the results. / The result will depend on the exam.
explanation: After the modal 'will' you need a base verb, but 'according to' is a preposition, not a verb. Either add a verb ('will decide/act according to ...') or restructure with a real verb such as 'depend on'.
correct_examples: The decision will depend on your score. | HR will act according to the results.
context: Explaining how a company technology exam result is used

my_sentence: It's just waste my time.
correction: It just wastes my time.
explanation: The subject 'it' takes the third-person singular verb 'wastes'. Don't insert 'is' before a plain present-tense action verb ('It's waste' = 'It is waste'), which is ungrammatical.
correct_examples: It just wastes my time. | This kind of exam only wastes our time.
context: Saying the exam was not useful for him

my_sentence: If someone tries to do that, he or she will be sent to prison for many years.
correction: If someone tries to do that, he or she can be sent to prison for many years.
explanation: Use 'can be' for a possible consequence/general rule rather than 'will be', which states it as a certainty. The penalty is a possibility, so 'can be sent' is more accurate.
correct_examples: Anyone who does that can be sent to prison. | Offenders can be jailed for several years.
context: Talking about gun laws and punishment in China

my_sentence: If you do it, you will get serious trouble.
correction: If you do it, you'll get into serious trouble.
explanation: The fixed collocation is 'get into trouble' — you need the preposition 'into'. 'Get serious trouble' is missing it.
correct_examples: You'll get into trouble if you do that. | He got into serious trouble with the police.
context: Consequences of owning a gun illegally

my_sentence: If you do not have permission to own a gun, you can be published up to three to seven years.
correction: If you do not have permission to own a gun, you can be jailed for three to seven years.
explanation: The word is 'punished'/'jailed', not 'published' (a confusable sound). Also, a length of time after a sentence takes 'for': 'jailed for X years', not 'up to ... years' without a preposition.
correct_examples: He can be jailed for up to seven years. | Offenders are punished with several years in prison.
context: Penalty for illegally owning a gun

my_sentence: The government wants to keep stability.
correction: The government wants to keep the stability of society. / The government wants to maintain social stability.
explanation: When 'stability' refers to a specific thing (society's), it needs the article + an 'of' phrase: 'the stability of society'. Alternatively use the adjective form 'social stability'.
correct_examples: They want to maintain the stability of society. | The policy is meant to preserve social stability.
context: Why the government restricts gun ownership

my_sentence: Police officers and soldiers can own the gun.
correction: Police officers and soldiers can own guns.
explanation: For a general statement about a category, use the bare plural 'guns', not 'the gun'. 'The gun' points to one specific gun.
correct_examples: Only soldiers can own guns. | Police officers are allowed to carry firearms.
context: Who is allowed to own a gun

my_sentence: If a lot of people arranged together, the government just needs to send five or ten officers.
correction: Even if hundreds of people gather together, they can be controlled by five to ten officers.
explanation: People come together = 'gather', not 'arrange'. 'Arrange' means to organize or put things in order; it does not mean a crowd assembling.
correct_examples: A crowd gathered in the square. | Even if thousands gather, the police can manage them.
context: Why an unarmed crowd is easy to control

my_sentence: If you have a gun, no one want to hurt you.
correction: If you have a gun, no one will want to hurt you. / no one wants to hurt you.
explanation: 'No one' is third-person singular, so it takes 'wants', or add the modal 'will' for a prediction: 'no one will want to'.
correct_examples: No one wants to be the last to leave. | If you are armed, no one will dare to attack you.
context: Arguing that owning a gun deters attackers

my_sentence: They are in the same level, no one is stronger than others.
correction: They are on the same level; no one is stronger than the others.
explanation: (assistant note) The collocation is 'on the same level', not 'in'. Also use 'the others' when referring to the rest of a known group.
correct_examples: With guns, everyone is on the same level. | The two teams are on the same level.
context: Claiming guns make everyone equal

my_sentence: For ordinary people, they don't allow to own guns.
correction: Ordinary people are not allowed to own guns.
explanation: Ordinary people are the ones being restricted, so use the passive 'are not allowed to'. The active 'they don't allow to' has no object and reverses who does the allowing.
correct_examples: Civilians are not allowed to carry weapons. | Students are not allowed to leave early.
context: Whether ordinary people in Japan can own guns

my_sentence: In the countryside, they don't have enough people.
correction: In the countryside, there aren't enough people.
explanation: To say something exists (or doesn't) in a place, use the existential 'there is/are', not 'they have'. 'They don't have' needs a specific possessor.
correct_examples: There aren't enough people in the village. | There were too many cars on the road.
context: Explaining firecrackers are fine in the countryside

my_sentence: My daughter wants to see some fireworks, so I bought some.
correction: My daughter wanted to see some fireworks, so I bought some.
explanation: Since you are narrating a past event (you already bought them), the first verb should also be past: 'wanted', to agree with 'bought'.
correct_examples: She wanted to watch the show, so I booked tickets. | He was hungry, so I made him a sandwich.
context: Why he bought fireworks years ago

my_sentence: My daughter wanted to see some fireworks, so I bought for her.
correction: My daughter wanted to see some fireworks, so I bought them for her.
explanation: 'Buy' is transitive here and needs a direct object: 'bought them'. Don't drop the object pronoun even when it's clear from context.
correct_examples: I bought them for her. | She liked the flowers, so he bought some for her.
context: Buying fireworks for his daughter

my_sentence: If you want to use them, you need to have a special processor.
correction: If you want to use them, you need to undergo a special process.
explanation: 'Process' is the procedure/steps; 'processor' is a device (e.g. a CPU). You mean the procedure, so use 'process'.
correct_examples: You must go through a special process to get a license. | The approval process takes two weeks.
context: Owning a collectible blade legally

my_sentence: They will become a sharpness.
correction: They will become sharp.
explanation: After 'become' use the adjective 'sharp' to describe a quality. 'Sharpness' is the noun form and takes no article here; 'a sharpness' is wrong.
correct_examples: The blade became sharp after grinding. | The picture became clear.
context: How a blade is processed to be sharp

my_sentence: Everyone need buy some knife to cut fruit and vegetables.
correction: Everyone needs to buy a knife to cut fruit and vegetables.
explanation: (assistant note) 'Everyone' is singular, so 'needs'; and 'need to' takes the to-infinitive: 'needs to buy'. Use the singular 'a knife' for one ordinary knife.
correct_examples: Everyone needs to buy a ticket. | She needs to take a knife to cut it.
context: Arguing that owning kitchen knives is normal

my_sentence: The government reviews the films before they show on TV or online.
correction: The government reviews the films before they are shown on TV or online.
explanation: (assistant note) The films are shown by someone, so use the passive 'are shown'. 'Before they show' would mean the films do the showing themselves.
correct_examples: The movie is reviewed before it is shown. | The data is checked before it is published.
context: How films are screened/censored before release

my_sentence: There are many films that was banned in my country.
correction: There are many films that were banned in my country.
explanation: (assistant note) The relative clause refers to 'films' (plural), so the verb must agree: 'that were banned'.
correct_examples: There are many books that were banned. | These are the films that were censored.
context: Listing banned violent films

my_sentence: There are too many violence in it, bloody and violence, so it was banned.
correction: There is too much violence and blood in it, so it was banned.
explanation: (assistant note) 'Violence' is uncountable, so use 'too much', not 'too many', and a singular verb 'there is'. Use the noun 'blood' (not 'bloody', an adjective) in the list of nouns.
correct_examples: There is too much violence in the film. | The movie has too much blood and gore.
context: Why a Jackie Chan film was banned in China

my_sentence: Being a firefighter has a higher salary than become a teacher.
correction: Being a firefighter can give you the chance to have a higher salary than becoming a teacher.
explanation: In a 'than' comparison the two things compared must have the same grammatical form. Since the first side is a gerund idea (being a firefighter), the second side must also be a gerund: 'than becoming a teacher', not the base verb 'become'.
correct_examples: Working in a big city pays more than working in a small town. | Running a company is harder than being an employee.
context: Comparing the pros and cons of being a firefighter vs. a teacher

my_sentence: If you as a firefighter work in a big city, you can earn about 10000 to 15000 every month.
correction: If you work as a firefighter in big cities, you can earn about 10000 to 15000 every month.
explanation: Keep the verb next to its subject: 'you work as a firefighter', not 'you as a firefighter work'. The role phrase 'as a firefighter' comes after the verb, and the place phrase 'in big cities' comes last.
correct_examples: If you work as a teacher in a big city, your salary is higher. | She works as a nurse in a large hospital.
context: Explaining how a firefighter's salary depends on city size

my_sentence: If you are not work for the government, but for local contract systems, you only earn about 4000 to 7000 every month.
correction: If you are not working for the government but for local contract systems, you only earn about 4000 to 7000 every month.
explanation: Don't put 'be' directly in front of a bare verb. Use either the present continuous 'are not working' or the simple present with 'do': 'if you do not work for the government'. 'are not work' is not a valid verb form.
correct_examples: If you are not working for the government, the pay is lower. | If you do not work for a state company, the benefits are fewer.
context: Describing firefighter pay under different employers

my_sentence: Your salary only can rent an apartment in the city center.
correction: You can only rent an apartment in the city center.
explanation: The adverb 'only' goes after the modal 'can', not before it: 'can only rent', not 'only can rent'. The word order for modal + adverb is subject + modal + adverb + main verb.
correct_examples: You can only afford a small place downtown. | He can only work part-time this month.
context: Saying a firefighter's salary is barely enough for rent in the city

my_sentence: I don't want to live a place like this.
correction: I don't want to live in a place like this.
explanation: 'live' as an intransitive verb needs the preposition 'in' before the place: 'live in a place', not 'live a place'. (You only drop the preposition when 'live' takes an adverb like 'here'.)
correct_examples: I don't want to live in a place like this. | They live in a quiet town by the sea.
context: Saying he wouldn't want to live in a crowded apartment building

my_sentence: Alibaba is in Hangzhou, so I went to there many times.
correction: Alibaba is in Hangzhou, so I have been there many times. / so I went there many times.
explanation: 'there' (and 'here') already act as adverbs of place, so they take no preposition: 'go there', not 'go to there'. For a repeated experience up to now, the present perfect 'I have been there many times' is more natural than the simple past.
correct_examples: I have been there many times. | We go there every summer.
context: Talking about visiting Alibaba's headquarters in Hangzhou

my_sentence: I lived in here after I graduated from college.
correction: I've lived here after I graduated. / I've lived here since I graduated.
explanation: Don't use 'in' before 'here'/'there'; say 'lived here', not 'lived in here'. Because you still live there now, use the present perfect 'I've lived here', and 'since I graduated' fits an ongoing state better than 'after'.
correct_examples: I've lived here since I graduated. | She has worked here for ten years.
context: Explaining why he settled in Yantai after college

my_sentence: Most children like to move from countryside to cities.
correction: Most children like to move from the countryside to the city.
explanation: 'the countryside' always takes 'the' (it refers to rural areas in general as a single place), and here 'the city' is used generically as its opposite. Say 'from the countryside to the city'.
correct_examples: Many young people move from the countryside to the city. | Life in the countryside is slower than life in the city.
context: Explaining why rural schools can't pay teachers well

my_sentence: I never thought working under Tencent.
correction: I have never thought of working under Tencent.
explanation: 'think' in this sense needs the preposition 'of' before the gerund: 'think of working'. Also use the present perfect 'I have never thought' for a life experience up to now.
correct_examples: I have never thought of working abroad. | Have you ever thought of starting your own business?
context: Answering whether he'd ever consider working for Tencent

my_sentence: I think it's a game company.
correction: I think it's a gaming company.
explanation: The natural collocation for a company that makes video games is 'gaming company' (or 'video game company'). Use the '-ing' form 'gaming' as the modifier here.
correct_examples: Tencent is a huge gaming company. | She works for a gaming studio.
context: Explaining why he wouldn't work for Tencent

my_sentence: They don't have any relative job. They are not related.
correction: They're not related.
explanation: To say two people are not family, English uses 'They're not related'. 'They don't have any relative job' is not idiomatic and mixes up 'relative' (a family member / connected) with 'related' (connected by family).
correct_examples: Jack Ma and Pony Ma are not related. | Are you two related?
context: Clarifying that Jack Ma and Pony Ma are not family

my_sentence: They are founder of their company.
correction: They are founders of their company.
explanation: When the subject is plural ('they' = two people) and each is a founder, the noun must be plural too: 'They are founders'.
correct_examples: They are the founders of the company. | We are the owners of this shop.
context: Talking about the founders of Alibaba and Tencent

my_sentence: If they are not belong to the government, they can have their own businesses.
correction: If they do not belong to the government, they can have their own businesses.
explanation: 'belong' is a stative verb used in the simple present, not with 'be'. Negate it with 'do': 'do not belong', never 'are not belong'.
correct_examples: This car does not belong to me. | If they do not belong to a union, they get no support.
context: Discussing whether firefighters can run their own businesses

my_sentence: They can have enough exercise every day and keep their body health.
correction: They can have enough exercise every day and keep their body healthy.
explanation: After 'keep + object' you need an adjective describing the object's state: 'keep their body healthy'. 'health' is the noun; 'healthy' is the adjective.
correct_examples: Exercise keeps your body healthy. | Try to keep the room clean.
context: Listing the pros of being a firefighter

my_sentence: There are many patients, so you don't need to worry about fired by the hospital.
correction: There are many patients, so you don't need to worry about getting fired by the hospital.
explanation: After the preposition 'about' you need a gerund. For a passive meaning use 'getting fired' (or 'being fired'), not the bare past participle 'fired'.
correct_examples: You don't have to worry about getting fired. | He's worried about being replaced.
context: Listing the pros of being a doctor (job security)

my_sentence: If you are a doctor, you can save many lives and you can earn the trust of them.
correction: If you are a doctor, you can save many lives and earn the trust of many people.
explanation: 'them' here has no clear plural noun to refer back to (you saved 'lives', not people, in the first clause), so it sounds vague. Name who trusts you: 'the trust of many people' (or 'their trust', if 'people' was already mentioned).
correct_examples: A good doctor earns the trust of many people. | You can win the trust of your patients.
context: Listing the pros of being a doctor

my_sentence: You need to face many patients every day, and you will be very busy and tiring.
correction: You need to face many patients every day, and you will be very busy and tired.
explanation: Use the '-ed' adjective for how a person feels ('tired') and the '-ing' adjective for the thing that causes the feeling ('tiring'). A person is tired; a job is tiring.
correct_examples: You are tired after a long shift. | This job is very tiring.
context: Listing the cons of being a doctor

my_sentence: If you make a small mistake, someone may lose his or her life.
correction: If you make a mistake, your patient may lose his or her life.
explanation: 'someone' is too vague for this context; the person at risk is specifically the patient. Naming 'your patient' makes the consequence clear and precise.
correct_examples: One small mistake and your patient could die. | If the pilot errs, the passengers are at risk.
context: Explaining the high stakes of a doctor's work

my_sentence: You need to explain the reason for their families.
correction: You need to explain the reason to his or her family.
explanation: You explain something TO the person who receives the explanation, not 'for' them. Use 'explain ... to someone'.
correct_examples: The doctor explained the situation to the family. | Let me explain the rules to you.
context: Talking about telling a patient's family bad news after surgery

my_sentence: My apartment overlooked the beach.
correction: My apartment is overlooking the beach. / My apartment overlooks the beach.
explanation: This is a permanent, present fact, so don't use the simple past 'overlooked' (which suggests it no longer does). Use the present: 'overlooks the beach' (simple present for a fixed fact) or 'is overlooking the beach'.
correct_examples: My apartment overlooks the sea. | The hotel room looks out over the harbor.
context: Describing where his apartment is, near the beach

my_sentence: It is about 6000 hospital in there.
correction: In my city, there are about 6000 hospitals.
explanation: A number greater than one takes a plural noun: '6000 hospitals'. To state that something exists, use the 'there are' structure ('there are about 6000 hospitals'), and drop the preposition before 'there' ('in there' -> just name the place).
correct_examples: There are about 6000 hospitals in my city. | There are ten students in the class.
context: Estimating the number of hospitals in Yantai

my_sentence: This hospital was built by the US, and the other hospital was built by the Japan.
correction: This hospital was built by the US, and the other was built by Japan.
explanation: Most country names take no article, so it's 'by Japan', not 'by the Japan'. (A few countries with plural or descriptive names, like 'the US' and 'the Philippines', do take 'the'.) Also 'the other' alone can stand for 'the other hospital'.
correct_examples: It was built by Japan after the war. | One was made in Japan, the other in Germany.
context: Talking about which countries built local hospitals

my_sentence: For me, I only gone to three famous hospitals.
correction: I have only been to three famous hospitals. / I have only gone to three famous hospitals.
explanation: The past participle 'gone' cannot stand alone as the main verb; it needs the auxiliary 'have' for the present perfect: 'I have gone / have been to'. For places you have visited and come back from, 'have been to' is the usual choice.
correct_examples: I have been to three hospitals in this city. | She has only been to Beijing once.
context: Saying which hospitals he has visited

my_sentence: But it's not cover all.
correction: But it does not cover all of it.
explanation: For a negative simple-present action verb you need the auxiliary 'do': 'it does not cover'. 'it's not cover' (= 'it is not cover') is ungrammatical because you can't put 'be' in front of a bare verb.
correct_examples: The insurance does not cover everything. | It doesn't cover the full cost.
context: Explaining that government medical insurance is limited

my_sentence: We are an developing country.
correction: We are a developing country.
explanation: Use 'a' before a consonant sound, not 'an'. 'developing' starts with the consonant /d/, so it is 'a developing country'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected in class, but a clear article error.)
correct_examples: China is a developing country. | It is a developing economy.
context: Explaining why the government can't cover all medical costs

my_sentence: If you work for college, your salary will higher than some people work in primary school.
correction: If you work at a college, your salary will be higher than that of people who work in primary school.
explanation: After the modal 'will' you still need 'be' before an adjective: 'will be higher', not 'will higher'. Also, to describe the people, add the relative pronoun 'who': 'people who work in primary school'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected in class, but both are clear errors.)
correct_examples: Your salary will be higher in a big city. | Teachers who work at universities usually earn more.
context: Explaining how teachers' salaries differ by school level

my_sentence: Not beef soup. Just a beef.
correction: Not beef soup. Just beef.
explanation: "Beef" is an uncountable noun, so it doesn't take the indefinite article "a" — say "beef," not "a beef."
correct_examples: I only had beef for dinner, not beef soup. | She eats chicken, not a chicken, for lunch.
context: Describing what he ate for dinner.

my_sentence: If you have a garden, you need to spend a lot of time to planter or fix something in it, like planting or watering and taking care of sunflowers.
correction: If you have a garden, you need to spend a lot of time planting, watering, and taking care of flowers.
explanation: "Spend time" is followed by a gerund (spend time doing something), not "to + verb"; also "planter" isn't an English verb — use "planting."
correct_examples: I spend a lot of time cleaning the house. | She spends hours watering her plants every week.
context: Talking about why he doesn't want a garden.

my_sentence: If they make a mistake, their patients will lose their lives. The patient can lose life.
correction: If they make a mistake, a patient can lose his or her life.
explanation: Use a possessive pronoun before "life" ("his or her life"); also refer to a general case with singular "a patient," not "their patients."
correct_examples: If the driver is careless, a passenger could lose his or her life. | One mistake, and a patient can lose his or her life.
context: Discussing the cons of being a doctor.

my_sentence: Do doctors need to be carefully?
correction: Doctors need to be careful.
explanation: After the linking verb "be," use the adjective "careful," not the adverb "carefully."
correct_examples: Please be careful when you cross the street. | You need to be careful with sharp knives.
context: Discussing doctors needing to be careful during operations.

my_sentence: There are too many patients who stayed in the hospital to look for an operation.
correction: There are too many patients who stayed in the hospital waiting for an operation.
explanation: "Look for" means to search for something; the intended meaning here is "waiting for," not searching.
correct_examples: Many patients are waiting for surgery. | He's been waiting for a bed in the hospital for days.
context: Discussing why doctors are overworked in big-city hospitals.

my_sentence: You can order a doctor in any time... I need to order a doctor in the city's hospital.
correction: You can make an appointment with a doctor at any time... I need to book an appointment with a doctor at the city's hospital.
explanation: (assistant note) The natural collocation for arranging a doctor's visit is "make/book an appointment," not "order a doctor."
correct_examples: I need to book an appointment with a specialist. | She made an appointment with her doctor for next week.
context: Talking about seeing doctors in different cities.

my_sentence: You don't need to wait a long time.
correction: You don't need to wait for a long time.
explanation: Use the preposition "for" before a duration with "wait": "wait for a long time."
correct_examples: We had to wait for a long time at the airport. | You won't have to wait for long.
context: Comparing the wait time to see a doctor in different cities.

my_sentence: They only live in big cities. It's a rarely can be found in small cities.
correction: It's rare that they cannot be found in small cities.
explanation: "Rare" is an adjective, not "a rarely" ("rarely" is an adverb and doesn't take the article "a"); build the sentence as "it's rare that + clause."
correct_examples: It's rare that you see snow here. | It's rare for experts to work in small towns.
context: Discussing the shortage of medical experts in small cities.

my_sentence: They want to live in big cities, not small cities.
correction: They want to live in big cities, not in the small one.
explanation: Avoid repeating the noun "cities" — replace it with a pronoun ("the small one"), as the teacher modeled, instead of repeating the noun.
correct_examples: I prefer the red one, not the blue one. | She lives in the countryside, not in the city.
context: Discussing why experts prefer big cities.

my_sentence: We can't find them in other places aside from the big one.
correction: We can't find them in other places aside from the big ones.
explanation: "Cities" is plural, so refer back to it with the plural "ones," not the singular "one."
correct_examples: Most stores are closed except the big ones. | I like all the photos except the small ones.
context: Continuing the discussion about where experts live.

my_sentence: Its change, it became, it became capital.
correction: It changed. It became the capital city.
explanation: Use the past-tense verb "changed," not the noun "change."
correct_examples: The city changed a lot over the years. | Beijing changed from a small town into a huge capital.
context: Discussing when Beijing became China's capital.

my_sentence: I think that's because the King want to protect the north part of China.
correction: I think that's because the king wanted to protect the northern part of China.
explanation: Use the past tense "wanted" to match the historical narrative, not the present "want."
correct_examples: The emperor wanted to defend the northern border. | They wanted to protect their territory.
context: Explaining why the capital moved to Beijing.

my_sentence: Wanted to protect the north part of China... it's the center of the south part of China.
correction: Wanted to protect the northern part of China... it's the center of the southern part of China.
explanation: Use the adjective forms "northern"/"southern" as modifiers before "part," not the bare directions "north"/"south."
correct_examples: He grew up in the southern part of the country. | The war started in the northern part of the region.
context: Discussing the geography of China's dynasties.

my_sentence: A astronaut can let people travel to space and see different planets.
correction: An astronaut can let people travel to space and see different planets.
explanation: Use "an" before a word starting with a vowel sound ("astronaut"), not "a."
correct_examples: An engineer designs new machines. | She wants to become an astronaut one day.
context: Talking about the pros of being an astronaut.

my_sentence: Being an astronaut can travel to space.
correction: Being an astronaut can let a person travel to space and see different planets.
explanation: "Being an astronaut" itself cannot "travel" — use a causative structure ("can let a person...") so the gerund phrase works correctly as the subject.
correct_examples: Being a doctor can let you help many people. | Being a pilot can let someone see the world.
context: Talking about the pros of being an astronaut.

my_sentence: Understand the word. (intending "understand the world")
correction: Understand the world.
explanation: "Word" and "world" differ by the "l" sound — the tip of the tongue must touch just behind the upper teeth to produce the "l" in "world," or it will sound like "word."
correct_examples: I want to see the world. | This changed my view of the world.
context: Practicing the pronunciation of "world" vs. "word."

my_sentence: They do some scientific research to help us understand the universal.
correction: They do some scientific research to help us understand the universe.
explanation: "Universe" is the noun (all of space); "universal" is an adjective meaning "applying to everything" — don't confuse the two.
correct_examples: Scientists study the universe. | This rule is a universal truth.
context: Discussing the pros of being an astronaut.

my_sentence: They do some scientific research out of space.
correction: They do some scientific research in outer space.
explanation: The correct phrase is "in outer space"; "out of space" means something else entirely ("no more room").
correct_examples: Astronauts conduct experiments in outer space. | The satellite orbits in outer space.
context: Discussing what astronauts do in space.

my_sentence: I mean for some people, he or she spent many years to form a habit.
correction: I mean, if they form a habit for many years, they can't change their ideas and behaviors easily.
explanation: Keep the generic subject pronoun consistent — use "they" throughout instead of switching to "he or she" mid-sentence.
correct_examples: If a person practices for years, they get very skilled. | Once someone forms a habit, they rarely break it easily.
context: Discussing whether criminals can change after seeing Earth from space.

my_sentence: I was very curious, so to explore something. It is a mystery.
correction: I was very curious about mysterious things.
explanation: Use the adjective form "mysterious things" as the object of "curious about," rather than saying "it is a mystery."
correct_examples: Children are often curious about mysterious things. | I've always been curious about ancient history.
context: Talking about his childhood dream of becoming an astronaut.

my_sentence: I needed to learn many thing.
correction: I needed to learn many things.
explanation: "Thing" needs the plural form "things" after "many."
correct_examples: We learned many things in school. | There are many things to consider.
context: Talking about growing up and forgetting his childhood dream.

my_sentence: I needed to learn many things at school and gradually I forgot my dream.
correction: I needed to learn many things at school and gradually I forgot all about it.
explanation: The more natural idiom here is "forgot all about it," rather than restating "forgot my dream."
correct_examples: He forgot all about his promise. | I forgot all about the meeting.
context: Talking about forgetting his childhood dream of becoming an astronaut.

my_sentence: Everyone can take a plan to go to travel out space.
correction: Everyone can plan to go to outer space.
explanation: "Plan" is used as a verb here ("plan to do something"), not as a noun with "take a plan"; also the phrase is "outer space," not "out space."
correct_examples: We plan to travel to Japan next year. | She plans to study abroad.
context: Speculating about ordinary people traveling to space in the future.

my_sentence: They need to train a lot for there.
correction: If someone wants to be an astronaut, he or she needs to train a lot for it.
explanation: Build the sentence with a clear conditional clause ("if someone wants to be an astronaut, ...") rather than a vague pronoun reference.
correct_examples: If someone wants to become a pilot, they need years of training. | If you want to be a doctor, you need to study hard.
context: Discussing the training required to become an astronaut.

my_sentence: You can easily to get.
correction: You can easily get it.
explanation: After the modal verb "can," use the bare infinitive (no "to"): "can easily get," not "can easily to get."
correct_examples: You can easily find it online. | She can easily solve this problem.
context: Discussing the pros of being a computer engineer (open-source resources).

my_sentence: You can easily get learn and communicate with them.
correction: You can easily learn and communicate with the authors.
explanation: Don't stack two verbs ("get learn"); just use the single verb "learn."
correct_examples: You can easily learn a new skill online. | He can easily learn new languages.
context: Discussing open-source resources in computer engineering.

my_sentence: If you produce a water, you only can sell it to one person.
correction: If you produce water, you can only sell it to one person.
explanation: "Water" is uncountable, so it doesn't take the indefinite article "a."
correct_examples: Factories produce water for the whole city. | This plant produces clean water.
context: Comparing physical products to software.

my_sentence: If you produce water, you only can sell it to one person.
correction: If you produce water, you can only sell it to one person.
explanation: The adverb "only" goes after the modal "can," not before it: "can only sell," not "only can sell."
correct_examples: You can only rent an apartment downtown. | He can only work part-time.
context: Comparing physical products to software.

my_sentence: You can sell it to hundreds of millions of people. (referring to software services)
correction: You can give services to hundreds of millions of people.
explanation: For software/services, the natural collocation is "give/provide services," not "sell," when emphasizing reach.
correct_examples: The app provides services to millions of users. | This platform gives services to people worldwide.
context: Discussing how software scales compared to physical products.

my_sentence: One person use one person usage, Tencent.
correction: One person's usage of Tencent Meeting.
explanation: Use the possessive apostrophe-s ("a person's usage") to show whose usage it is, not the bare noun.
correct_examples: This is my sister's car. | The company's profit doubled this year.
context: Discussing the cost of a person using Tencent Meeting.

my_sentence: If a person uses the Tencent Meeting, the cost is the same four hundreds of people.
correction: If a person uses Tencent Meeting, the cost is the same for hundreds of people.
explanation: "For" (preposition) and "four" (number) are homophones — the intended word here is "for," not "four."
correct_examples: This price is the same for everyone. | The plan works for hundreds of users.
context: Explaining that software scales at the same cost.

my_sentence: The cost for a person and 100 people to use Tencent Meeting is the same.
correction: The cost for a person and hundreds of people who use Tencent Meeting is the same.
explanation: Use the relative pronoun "who" to introduce the clause describing the people, rather than an infinitive "to use."
correct_examples: The teacher who taught us math retired. | People who use this app love it.
context: Explaining the cost structure of software services.

my_sentence: The Tencent Company deploys their services like the Tencent Meeting, who needs to...
correction: The Tencent Company deploys their services like the Tencent Meeting, that needs to...
explanation: Use "that/which" for things (like an application), not "who," which is reserved for people.
correct_examples: The app, which needs an update, crashed yesterday. | This is the software that needs fixing.
context: Explaining how companies deploy software services.

my_sentence: If you want to deploy your services, you need to prepare some virtual machines.
correction: If you want to give your services, you need to deploy your application on some electronic devices.
explanation: "Deploy" collocates with "application" (the technical setup), while "give/provide" collocates with "services" — don't swap them.
correct_examples: We deployed the new application last night. | The company provides services to thousands of customers.
context: Explaining how to run an online service.

my_sentence: Virtual machines like computers or laptops.
correction: Electronic devices like computers or laptops.
explanation: "Virtual machine" is a specific technical term (a software-based computer); for general physical devices, use "electronic devices."
correct_examples: You need electronic devices like a phone or laptop. | The store sells many electronic devices.
context: Explaining what's needed to run an application.

my_sentence: This idea to. Or so naive. (intending "their ideas are so naive")
correction: Their ideas are so naive.
explanation: Use the plural possessive "their ideas," matching the plural referent, not the singular "this idea."
correct_examples: Their plans are too naive. | His ideas are still very simple.
context: Discussing the cons of being a computer engineer (lack of experience).

my_sentence: They don't know many in the world.
correction: They don't know much about the world.
explanation: (assistant note) "Many" is for countable nouns; the intended meaning ("not much knowledge") needs "much" and the phrase "about the world."
correct_examples: He doesn't know much about cooking. | I don't know much about finance.
context: Discussing why new engineers seem naive.

my_sentence: If some of them meet any problem, they don't know how to fix it.
correction: If any of them meets some problems, they don't know how to fix them.
explanation: With "any of them" as the subject, the verb should agree in the third-person singular ("meets"), and plural "problems" pairs naturally with "some."
correct_examples: If any of them needs help, let me know. | If any of the students has questions, raise your hand.
context: Discussing coworkers who struggle to communicate with strangers.

my_sentence: If they don't try to strive for their interests, no one will consider for them.
correction: If they don't try to strive for their interests, no one will consider them.
explanation: "Consider" takes a direct object without a preposition ("consider them"), not "consider for them."
correct_examples: The manager will consider your request. | No one considered my opinion.
context: Discussing self-interest in the workplace.

my_sentence: We learned many things about sharing and considering others during our childhood, but reality, one way, when we face some interest, we just consider for our own.
correction: We learned many things about sharing and considering others during our childhood, but in reality, when we follow our own interests, we want to get more.
explanation: The fixed phrase is "in reality" (with the preposition "in"), not "reality" alone.
correct_examples: In theory it sounds simple, but in reality it's hard. | In reality, things rarely go as planned.
context: Discussing self-interest versus childhood values.

my_sentence: When we share some interests together, we want to get more.
correction: When we follow our own interests together, we want to get more.
explanation: The intended meaning here is "follow one's own interests" (pursue what benefits you), not "share interests."
correct_examples: Everyone follows their own interests at work. | He always follows his own interests first.
context: Discussing self-interest in group work.

my_sentence: Someone can get more and others can get less.
correction: Someone can get more and others have less.
explanation: Avoid repeating the same verb ("get") in parallel clauses — vary it ("get more... have less") for natural style.
correct_examples: Some win big and others lose a little. | Some earn a lot and others make very little.
context: Discussing unequal sharing of profits in a team.

my_sentence: I think the society is so complexity.
correction: I think society is so complex.
explanation: After "is," use the adjective "complex," not the noun "complexity."
correct_examples: Modern life is very complex. | This problem is more complex than it looks.
context: Discussing competition and cooperation in society.

my_sentence: Whenever I go out, I was customer.
correction: Whenever I go out, I will be a customer.
explanation: Use the future tense "will be" for this general/habitual scenario, not the past "was," and include the article "a" before the singular noun "customer."
correct_examples: Whenever I travel, I will be a tourist there. | When I visit that store, I will be a customer.
context: Talking about buying products and services.

my_sentence: I spend money to buy services.
correction: I spend my own money to get some services.
explanation: The natural collocation here is "get services" (or "get a service"), not "buy services."
correct_examples: I paid to get a haircut. | We get cleaning services once a week.
context: Talking about spending money as a customer.

my_sentence: So I smile a lot often.
correction: So I smile a lot more often.
explanation: "A lot" can intensify a comparative ("a lot more often"), but it can't just sit next to "often" without the comparative "more" connecting them.
correct_examples: I exercise more often now. | She visits her parents more often since moving closer.
context: Talking about smiling more when spending time with family.

my_sentence: I am not familiar about everyone around my neighborhood.
correction: I am not familiar with everyone around my neighborhood.
explanation: (assistant note) "Familiar" is followed by the preposition "with," not "about."
correct_examples: I'm not familiar with this software. | She is familiar with the local customs.
context: Talking about not knowing his neighbors well.

my_sentence: As a patient or as ordinary people, you don't know anything about medicine.
correction: As a patient or as an ordinary person, you don't know anything about medicine.
explanation: Keep the parallel structure singular ("a patient... an ordinary person"), matching number on both sides of "or."
correct_examples: As a student or as a teacher, you have responsibilities. | As a parent or as an employee, time management matters.
context: Discussing patients' rights to ask doctors questions.

my_sentence: You don't know anything about medicine, anything about medicine or processes.
correction: You don't know many things about medicine or its processes.
explanation: Avoid repeating "anything about medicine," and use the possessive "its processes" to refer back to "medicine."
correct_examples: I don't know much about this company or its history. | She doesn't understand chemistry or its formulas.
context: Discussing patients' lack of medical knowledge.

my_sentence: If she change a new one, it's...
correction: If she changed to a new company, ...
explanation: "Change" needs the preposition "to" before the destination ("change to a new company"), and the past tense "changed" fits this hypothetical scenario.
correct_examples: He changed to a different job last year. | She changed to a new school in September.
context: Discussing an employee who couldn't get promoted.

my_sentence: I don't think it's a short years. Three to five years are so long.
correction: I don't think it's a short time. Three to five years is a long time.
explanation: (assistant note) Don't use the singular article "a" with the plural noun "years" — say "a short time," or drop the article and keep "years" plural without "a."
correct_examples: Two years is a long time to wait. | That's not a short time to spend on one project.
context: Discussing how long a delay in promotion feels.

my_sentence: You don't get promotion.
correction: You don't get promoted.
explanation: After "get" (meaning "become"), use the past participle "promoted," not the noun "promotion."
correct_examples: She got promoted last month. | If you work hard, you'll get promoted.
context: Explaining what happens if you stay at the same level for years.

my_sentence: You will feel terrible and unfair.
correction: You will feel terrible about it and think it's unfair.
explanation: "Unfair" describes a judgment, not a feeling — say "think it's unfair" rather than "feel unfair."
correct_examples: He felt bad about the decision and thought it was unjust. | I feel upset about it and think it's not right.
context: Discussing how it feels to be passed over for a promotion.

my_sentence: Today I did some tasks in my work.
correction: Today I did some tasks at work.
explanation: Use the fixed phrase "at work" for being at one's job, not "in my work."
correct_examples: I was at work all day. | She stayed late at work yesterday.
context: Describing his day when asked what he did.

my_sentence: I think most of the authors willing to share their ideas and method to you for free.
correction: I think most of the authors are willing to share their ideas and methods with you for free.
explanation: The adjective "willing" needs the auxiliary "be" ("are willing") to form a complete verb group — it can't follow the subject directly.
correct_examples: Most teachers are willing to help after class. | The company is willing to negotiate the price.
context: Discussing the open-source culture in software engineering.
